  1. The limitation clauses (§8 of Human Dignity and Liberty; §4 of Freedom of Occupation) textually authorize judicial review of legislation; without judicial enforcement, the clauses are meaningless
  2. The Knesset’s own legislative practice — passing Basic Laws under more demanding procedural rules, citing them as constitutional norms in subsequent debates — implies self-understanding as constituent assembly
  3. The basic-structure doctrine extended in 2024 has parallels (India, Bangladesh, Slovakia) and is necessary to prevent constitutional abuse via simple-majority Basic Law amendments

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh, and Kontorovich, holds:

  1. The 1992 Basic Laws passed with tiny majorities (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present) — inconsistent with the claim that the Knesset self-understood itself as enacting a constitution
  2. The Bank Mizrahi framework is judicial self-assertion — the Court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate
  3. The 2024 extension to Basic Law amendments is without precedent in any democratic system that lacks a codified constitution; the comparator democracies all have codified constitutions providing the authorization

This explainer documents the framework and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Versa / Cardozo — United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village
  • Versa / Cardozo — Full English PDF of Bank Mizrahi
  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy” — Law & Liberty
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat — “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Reversing the Constitutional Revolution
  • Bar-Ilan Law — Constitutional Revolutions: Israel as a Case Study
explainers.body_html_en:
Why this concept matters

Israel does not have a single written constitution. Instead, the Knesset, since 1958, has passed a series of Basic Laws (חוקי-יסוד) covering different aspects of governmental structure and rights — currently 13 in total. The intention, per the 1950 Harari Resolution, was that these would eventually be combined into a complete written constitution.

The Basic Laws have a special status: they are passed as ordinary statutes, but the Israeli Supreme Court has treated them as constitutional-rank texts, holding ordinary legislation that conflicts with them as invalid. This means the Knesset, when it enacts a Basic Law, is treated as acting in two roles simultaneously — as ordinary legislator AND as constituent assembly (רשות מכוננת) creating constitutional norms.

The legal-doctrinal foundation for this dual role was established in 1995. The extension of judicial review to amendments of Basic Laws themselves was established in 2024.

The 1995 foundation: Bank Mizrahi

In CA 6821/93 United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village (9 November 1995), the Supreme Court — sitting as a nine-justice panel for what was nominally a civil appeal about agricultural debt — articulated the foundational doctrine.

President Aharon Barak’s lead opinion held:

  • The Knesset acts in two capacities: as ordinary legislator (passing statutes) and as constituent assembly (creating Basic Laws of constitutional rank)
  • This dual capacity transferred from the First Knesset, which Harari Resolution authorized as a constitution-drafter, to every subsequent Knesset through institutional continuity
  • The Basic Laws of 1992 (Human Dignity and Liberty; Freedom of Occupation), and their limitation clauses (§8 and §4 respectively), provide the textual authorization for judicial review of ordinary legislation
  • The source of constituent authority is popular sovereignty rather than parliamentary sovereignty

This was a substantial doctrinal pronouncement. As Cohen-Eliya notes in Israel’s Juristocracy, Barak’s framework “radically expanded the significance of the 1992 Basic Laws, far beyond any intention contemplated by the legislators who voted for them.” Barak’s argument, in his earlier 1976 formulation that he later operationalized as President, was that judges are “the vanguard of the aspiration for the better, more desirable law… the architects of social change.”

A critical detail often missed

The Bank Mizrahi ruling — despite being the foundation of all subsequent constitutional review of ordinary legislation in Israel — did not actually strike down any law. The Court upheld the Family Agricultural Sector (Arrangements) Law Amendment that was challenged. The doctrinal architecture (Knesset’s constituent authority, Basic Laws as supreme constitution, judicial power to invalidate statutes) was articulated in obiter dicta — pronouncements made in the course of the judgment but not strictly necessary to its disposition.

The first actual statutory invalidation under the Bank Mizrahi framework came two years later, in Investment Managers Bureau (1997).

Whether the Bank Mizrahi pronouncement counts as binding holding or as persuasive dicta is itself a battleground in the post-2023 debate. Defenders treat it as the ratio of a 30-year-stable jurisprudence; critics (Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh) treat it as a judicial self-bootstrapping that subsequent precedent has papered over.

The 2024 extension: judicial review of Basic Law amendments

Bank Mizrahi authorized the Court to strike down ordinary legislation that conflicts with Basic Laws. The 2024 ruling extended this to Basic Law amendments themselves.

In HCJ 5658/23 (1 January 2024), the Court — sitting en banc as a 15-justice panel — issued two distinct rulings:

  • 12-3: The Court has authority, in principle, to invalidate amendments to Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority
  • 8-7: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary (the “reasonableness amendment”) is invalidated as exceeding that authority

President Hayut’s lead opinion adopted a framework analogous to India’s “basic structure” doctrine: the Knesset’s constituent authority is not unlimited, and amendments that deny “the core characteristics of the State of Israel as a Jewish and democratic state” are reviewable.

The full structured record of HCJ 5658/23, including the panel composition and the vote breakdowns, is in data/rulings/5658-23.json.

What this means structurally

The combination of Bank Mizrahi (1995) and HCJ 5658/23 (2024) gives the Israeli Supreme Court an unusual position by comparative-constitutional standards:

  1. The Court reviews ordinary legislation against Basic Laws (since 1995)
  2. The Court reviews amendments of Basic Laws against doctrinal limits the Court itself articulates (since 2024)
  3. The Court reviews appointment to the Court through the Judicial Selection Committee’s supermajority rule (since 2008)
  4. The Court reviews executive decisions under the broadly-construed reasonableness doctrine (since the 1980s)

Cohen-Eliya argues in Israel’s Juristocracy that this combination is “no precedent in the democratic world” — that no other democratic system has accrued comparable judicial power without explicit constitutional authorization. India and Bangladesh have analogous basic-structure doctrines, but both operate within fully codified constitutions. Israel’s doctrine operates without one.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the constituent-authority framework — Barak himself, Daphne Barak-Erez (now a sitting justice), and scholars like Yoav Dotan — argue:

  1. The limitation clauses (§8 of Human Dignity and Liberty; §4 of Freedom of Occupation) textually authorize judicial review of legislation; without judicial enforcement, the clauses are meaningless
  2. The Knesset’s own legislative practice — passing Basic Laws under more demanding procedural rules, citing them as constitutional norms in subsequent debates — implies self-understanding as constituent assembly
  3. The basic-structure doctrine extended in 2024 has parallels (India, Bangladesh, Slovakia) and is necessary to prevent constitutional abuse via simple-majority Basic Law amendments

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh, and Kontorovich, holds:

  1. The 1992 Basic Laws passed with tiny majorities (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present) — inconsistent with the claim that the Knesset self-understood itself as enacting a constitution
  2. The Bank Mizrahi framework is judicial self-assertion — the Court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate
  3. The 2024 extension to Basic Law amendments is without precedent in any democratic system that lacks a codified constitution; the comparator democracies all have codified constitutions providing the authorization

This explainer documents the framework and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Versa / Cardozo — United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village
  • Versa / Cardozo — Full English PDF of Bank Mizrahi
  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy” — Law & Liberty
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat — “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Reversing the Constitutional Revolution
  • Bar-Ilan Law — Constitutional Revolutions: Israel as a Case Study

explainers.title: The Judicial Selection Committee (הוועדה לבחירת שופטים)
explainers.summary: How Israel's Judicial Selection Committee is structured, the supermajority rule that effectively gives the sitting Court a veto over its successors, and why this body became the central focus of post-Jan-2024 reform efforts.
explainers.summary_he: כיצד בנויה הוועדה הישראלית לבחירת שופטים, מהו כלל הרוב המיוחס שנותן הלכה למעשה לבית המשפט היושב על המדוכה זכות וטו על יורשיו, ומדוע הפך מוסד זה למוקד המרכזי של מאמצי הרפורמה לאחר ינואר 2024.
explainers.summary_en: How Israel's Judicial Selection Committee is structured, the supermajority rule that effectively gives the sitting Court a veto over its successors, and why this body became the central focus of post-Jan-2024 reform efforts.
explainers.body_html:
What it is

The Judicial Selection Committee is the statutory body that appoints all judges in Israel — Supreme Court, District, and Magistrate. It is established under Basic Law: The Judiciary and operates per the Courts Law and supplementary regulations.

Unlike most democracies, where senior judges are appointed by the elected executive (often with legislative confirmation), Israel’s Committee gives sitting judges and the Bar Association significant institutional weight in selecting their colleagues and successors. This composition — and the supermajority rule that operates on top of it — is the structural mechanism around which most of the 2023-2025 reform debate turned.

Composition (as of mid-2026)

The Committee has nine members:

Members Source
3 Supreme Court justices Court itself; includes the Supreme Court President
2 Bar Association representatives Israel Bar Association
2 Ministers Cabinet (one is the Justice Minister, who chairs)
2 Knesset Members Knesset (by convention, 1 coalition + 1 opposition)

This composition has been adjusted in different ways over the years, but the 3-judge / 2-bar / 4-political balance has been the long-standing default.

The supermajority rule for Supreme Court appointments

Since a 2008 reform, Supreme Court appointments require a supermajority of 7 out of 9 members. This means:

  • The 3 sitting justices, voting together, plus any one additional member (a Bar rep, an MK, or a minister), can block any appointment they oppose
  • Equivalently, no Supreme Court appointment can succeed against the unified opposition of the sitting justices

The supermajority rule was, at the time of its 2008 enactment, presented as a measure to ensure broad consensus on appointments. In practice, it has functioned as something closer to a veto power for the sitting Court over its successors — at least when the sitting justices vote as a bloc.

This is the structural feature that Cohen-Eliya and other reform-aligned scholars identify as the operating mechanism of what they call the “juristocracy”: the Court’s institutional capacity to perpetuate its own composition through the supermajority gate.

The seniority convention for Court President

By convention (not statute), the most senior sitting justice becomes the next President of the Supreme Court upon the prior President’s retirement. The Committee formally elects the President, but in practice the choice is determined by seniority.

This convention came under stress in 2023-2025 when Justice Minister Yariv Levin refused to convene the Committee for ~16 months after Esther Hayut’s October 2023 retirement, in order to block the elevation of Yitzhak Amit (next in seniority).

The High Court of Justice ultimately ordered the Committee convened, and Amit was elected President in January 2025 — with the coalition members boycotting both the vote and the subsequent swearing-in ceremony. See data/rulings/ for related documentary entries.

The March 2025 reconstitution law

On 27 March 2025, the Knesset passed a law restructuring the Committee’s composition:

  • The 2 Bar Association representatives were removed
  • 2 new positions were added: 1 lawyer chosen by the coalition, 1 lawyer chosen by the opposition

This shifts the balance toward political control of the Committee, though less aggressively than the original January 2023 Levin-Rothman proposal (which would have given the coalition automatic majority).

The new law takes effect at the start of the next Knesset — meaning it doesn’t apply to current Committee operations but will reshape future appointments.

Why this is the central post-2024 reform battleground

After the January 2024 Supreme Court ruling striking down the reasonableness amendment, the broader reform package was effectively dead. The coalition pivoted from direct constraint of the Court’s powers to changing who sits on the Court through Committee restructuring.

The logic is the same logic that Cohen-Eliya identifies in Israel’s Juristocracy: the Court’s authority and the Committee’s composition are mutually reinforcing. The Court’s authority over Knesset legislation depends on the Court’s composition; the Committee’s composition controls the Court’s composition; the supermajority rule gives the Court itself a veto over the Committee’s composition decisions.

Breaking this circuit, in the reform-aligned view, is necessary to restore democratic accountability to the appointment of judges. In the defenders’ view, breaking this circuit risks turning the Court into a partisan instrument and undermining judicial independence.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the existing supermajority structure — including Prof. Suzie Navot (Reichman / IDI), Prof. Mordechai Kremnitzer (IDI senior fellow), and the institutional position of the Israel Democracy Institute — argue:

  1. Judicial independence requires insulation from majoritarian politics. Most democracies that achieve judicial independence do so by removing senior judicial appointments from the immediate electoral cycle; the 2008 supermajority reform was precisely such an insulation mechanism, and reversing it would re-introduce the cyclical politicization Israel deliberately moved away from.
  2. Supermajority requirements ensure broad consensus across political shifts. A 7-of-9 threshold means a justice cannot be appointed (or blocked) on the strength of a thin coalition’s preferences; this stabilizes the Court’s composition against the unusually high turnover of Israeli governments.
  3. The “sitting-Court veto” framing overstates the empirical record. Defenders note that in the years since 2008, the Committee has appointed justices across the ideological spectrum, including appointees viewed as conservative (Sohlberg, Mintz, Elron). The supermajority gate has not, in practice, perpetuated a single ideological bloc.
  4. Removing the Bar reps (in the 2025 law) reduces professional input in favor of pure politics — the Bar’s institutional position is meant to weight the professional-competence consideration above ideological alignment, and political-only selection would erode that.
  5. Comparative-constitutional argument: most consolidated democracies use supermajority or counter-majoritarian mechanisms for senior judicial appointments. Israel’s 2008 model is structurally closer to the international consensus than to outlier majoritarian designs.

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Koppel, and Rothman, holds that: 1. Independence from politics is not the same as immunity from democratic legitimacy 2. The supermajority “consensus” requirement in practice gives the sitting Court an over-representative voice in its own succession 3. Bar Association representation gives outsized weight to a professional guild whose interests do not necessarily align with broader public preferences

This explainer documents the structure and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Wikipedia — Judicial Selection Committee (Israel)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — How Israeli Judges Are Appointed
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Ministers Levin and Sa’ar’s Proposed Changes to the Judicial Selection Committee
  • Lawfare — Israel’s Renewed Judicial Overhaul
  • Times of Israel — Judicial appointments law is Levin’s victory
explainers.body_html_he:
מהי

הוועדה לבחירת שופטים היא הגוף הסטטוטורי הממנה את כל השופטים בישראל — בבית המשפט העליון, בבתי המשפט המחוזיים ובבתי משפט השלום. היא מעוגנת בחוק-יסוד: השפיטה ופועלת על פי חוק בתי המשפט ותקנות משלימות.

בשונה מרוב הדמוקרטיות, שבהן שופטים בכירים ממונים על ידי הרשות המבצעת הנבחרת (לעיתים קרובות באישור הרשות המחוקקת), הוועדה הישראלית מקנה לשופטים המכהנים וללשכת עורכי הדין משקל מוסדי ניכר בבחירת עמיתיהם ויורשיהם. הרכב זה — וכלל הרוב המיוחס הפועל מעליו — הוא המנגנון המבני שסביבו נסבה מרבית מחלוקת הרפורמה של השנים 2023–2025.

הרכב (נכון לאמצע 2026)

לוועדה תשעה חברים:

חברים מקור
3 שופטי בית המשפט העליון בית המשפט עצמו; כולל את נשיא בית המשפט העליון
2 נציגי לשכת עורכי הדין לשכת עורכי הדין בישראל
2 שרים הממשלה (אחד מהם שר המשפטים, המכהן כיו”ר)
2 חברי כנסת הכנסת (על פי המנהג, 1 מהקואליציה + 1 מהאופוזיציה)

הרכב זה הותאם בדרכים שונות לאורך השנים, אך האיזון של 3 שופטים / 2 נציגי לשכה / 4 נציגים פוליטיים היה ברירת המחדל הוותיקה.

כלל הרוב המיוחס למינויים לבית המשפט העליון

מאז רפורמת 2008, מינויים לבית המשפט העליון מחייבים רוב מיוחס של 7 מתוך 9 חברים. משמעות הדבר:

  • 3 השופטים המכהנים, המצביעים יחד, בתוספת כל חבר אחד נוסף (נציג לשכה, חבר כנסת או שר), יכולים לחסום כל מינוי שהם מתנגדים לו
  • באופן שקול, אף מינוי לבית המשפט העליון אינו יכול לצלוח כנגד התנגדות מאוחדת של השופטים המכהנים

כלל הרוב המיוחס הוצג בעת חקיקתו ב-2008 כצעד שנועד להבטיח הסכמה רחבה על מינויים. בפועל, הוא תפקד כדבר הקרוב יותר לזכות וטו של בית המשפט המכהן על יורשיו — לפחות כאשר השופטים המכהנים מצביעים כגוש אחד.

זהו המאפיין המבני שכהן-אליה ומלומדים נוספים מקרב מצדדי הרפורמה מזהים כמנגנון הפעולה של מה שהם מכנים “יוריסטוקרטיה”: יכולתו המוסדית של בית המשפט להנציח את הרכבו שלו דרך שער הרוב המיוחס.

מנהג הוותק לבחירת נשיא בית המשפט

על פי מנהג (ולא על פי חוק), השופט הוותיק ביותר מבין המכהנים הופך לנשיא הבא של בית המשפט העליון עם פרישת הנשיא הקודם. הוועדה בוחרת רשמית את הנשיא, אך בפועל הבחירה נקבעת על פי ותק.

מנהג זה עמד במבחן בשנים 2023–2025, כאשר שר המשפטים יריב לוין סירב לכנס את הוועדה במשך כ-16 חודשים לאחר פרישתה של אסתר חיות באוקטובר 2023, במטרה לחסום את העלאתו של יצחק עמית (הוותיק הבא בתור).

בג”ץ הורה בסופו של דבר לכנס את הוועדה, ועמית נבחר לנשיא בינואר 2025 — כאשר חברי הקואליציה החרימו הן את ההצבעה והן את טקס ההשבעה שלאחריה. ראו data/rulings/ לרשומות תיעודיות נלוות.

חוק עיצוב הוועדה מחדש ממרץ 2025

ב-27 במרץ 2025 העבירה הכנסת חוק המשנה את הרכב הוועדה:

  • 2 נציגי לשכת עורכי הדין הוסרו
  • נוספו 2 תפקידים חדשים: עורך דין אחד שנבחר על ידי הקואליציה, ועורך דין אחד שנבחר על ידי האופוזיציה

שינוי זה מסיט את האיזון לכיוון שליטה פוליטית בוועדה, אם כי באופן פחות אגרסיבי מהצעת לוין-רוטמן המקורית מינואר 2023 (שהייתה מקנה לקואליציה רוב אוטומטי).

החוק החדש ייכנס לתוקף בתחילת כהונתה של הכנסת הבאה — כלומר אינו חל על פעילות הוועדה הנוכחית, אך יעצב מחדש את המינויים העתידיים.

מדוע זוהי זירת ההתגוששות המרכזית של הרפורמה שלאחר 2024

לאחר פסיקת בית המשפט העליון מינואר 2024 שפסלה את תיקון הסבירות, חבילת הרפורמה הרחבה הייתה למעשה מתה. הקואליציה עברה מהגבלה ישירה של סמכויות בית המשפט אל שינוי זהות היושבים בבית המשפט באמצעות עיצוב הוועדה מחדש.

ההיגיון זהה להיגיון שכהן-אליה מזהה בIsrael’s Juristocracy: סמכותו של בית המשפט והרכב הוועדה מחזקים זה את זה הדדית. סמכות בית המשפט על חקיקת הכנסת תלויה בהרכב בית המשפט; הרכב הוועדה שולט בהרכב בית המשפט; וכלל הרוב המיוחס מעניק לבית המשפט עצמו זכות וטו על החלטות הוועדה בדבר הרכבה.

ניתוק מעגל זה, על פי גישת מצדדי הרפורמה, הכרחי כדי להשיב אחריותיות דמוקרטית למינוי השופטים. על פי גישת המתנגדים, ניתוק מעגל זה מסכן בהפיכת בית המשפט לכלי מפלגתי ובפגיעה בעצמאות השיפוטית.

הערה על הוגנות אדוורסרית

מצדדי מבנה הרוב המיוחס הקיים — ובהם פרופ’ סוזי נבות (רייכמן / המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה), פרופ’ מרדכי קרמניצר (עמית בכיר במכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה) והעמדה המוסדית של המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה — טוענים:

  1. עצמאות שיפוטית מחייבת בידוד מן הפוליטיקה הרובנית. מרבית הדמוקרטיות המשיגות עצמאות שיפוטית עושות זאת באמצעות הוצאת מינויים שיפוטיים בכירים ממחזור הבחירות המיידי; רפורמת הרוב המיוחס של 2008 הייתה בדיוק מנגנון בידוד מסוג זה, וביטולה יחזיר את הפוליטיזציה המחזורית שישראל התרחקה ממנה במכוון.
  2. דרישות רוב מיוחס מבטיחות הסכמה רחבה על פני תהפוכות פוליטיות. רף של 7 מתוך 9 פירושו ששופט אינו יכול להתמנות (או להיחסם) על בסיס העדפותיה של קואליציה דקה; הדבר מייצב את הרכב בית המשפט אל מול התחלופה הגבוהה באופן חריג של ממשלות ישראל.
  3. מסגור “וטו בית המשפט המכהן” מפריז ביחס לתיעוד האמפירי. המצדדים מציינים כי בשנים שחלפו מאז 2008 מינתה הוועדה שופטים מכל קצות הקשת האידיאולוגית, ובכללם ממונים הנחשבים שמרנים (סולברג, מינץ, אלרון). שער הרוב המיוחס לא הנציח בפועל גוש אידיאולוגי יחיד.
  4. הסרת נציגי הלשכה (בחוק 2025) מצמצמת את התשומה המקצועית לטובת פוליטיקה גרידא — מעמדה המוסדי של הלשכה נועד לתת משקל לשיקול הכשירות המקצועית מעל ההלימה האידיאולוגית, ובחירה פוליטית בלבד תכרסם בכך.
  5. טיעון משפטי-חוקתי משווה: מרבית הדמוקרטיות המבוססות נוקטות מנגנוני רוב מיוחס או מנגנונים נוגדי-רוב למינויים שיפוטיים בכירים. מודל 2008 הישראלי קרוב מבחינה מבנית לקונצנזוס הבינלאומי יותר מאשר לתבניות רובניות חריגות.

עמדת מצדדי הרפורמה, כפי שמנוסחת על ידי כהן-אליה, קופל ורוטמן, גורסת כי: 1. עצמאות מן הפוליטיקה אינה זהה לחסינות מפני לגיטימציה דמוקרטית 2. דרישת ה”הסכמה” של הרוב המיוחס מקנה בפועל לבית המשפט המכהן קול חורג מהפרופורציה בקביעת יורשיו 3. ייצוג לשכת עורכי הדין מעניק משקל חורג מהפרופורציה לגילדה מקצועית שאינטרסיה אינם בהכרח עולים בקנה אחד עם העדפות הציבור הרחב

מאמר הסבר זה מתעד את המבנה ואת המחלוקת; הוא אינו מכריע מי משתי העמדות צודקת.

קריאה נוספת

  • Wikipedia — Judicial Selection Committee (Israel)
  • המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה — כיצד ממונים שופטים בישראל
  • המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה — השינויים שמציעים השרים לוין וסער בוועדה לבחירת שופטים
  • Lawfare — Israel’s Renewed Judicial Overhaul
  • Times of Israel — Judicial appointments law is Levin’s victory
explainers.body_html_en:
What it is

The Judicial Selection Committee is the statutory body that appoints all judges in Israel — Supreme Court, District, and Magistrate. It is established under Basic Law: The Judiciary and operates per the Courts Law and supplementary regulations.

Unlike most democracies, where senior judges are appointed by the elected executive (often with legislative confirmation), Israel’s Committee gives sitting judges and the Bar Association significant institutional weight in selecting their colleagues and successors. This composition — and the supermajority rule that operates on top of it — is the structural mechanism around which most of the 2023-2025 reform debate turned.

Composition (as of mid-2026)

The Committee has nine members:

Members Source
3 Supreme Court justices Court itself; includes the Supreme Court President
2 Bar Association representatives Israel Bar Association
2 Ministers Cabinet (one is the Justice Minister, who chairs)
2 Knesset Members Knesset (by convention, 1 coalition + 1 opposition)

This composition has been adjusted in different ways over the years, but the 3-judge / 2-bar / 4-political balance has been the long-standing default.

The supermajority rule for Supreme Court appointments

Since a 2008 reform, Supreme Court appointments require a supermajority of 7 out of 9 members. This means:

  • The 3 sitting justices, voting together, plus any one additional member (a Bar rep, an MK, or a minister), can block any appointment they oppose
  • Equivalently, no Supreme Court appointment can succeed against the unified opposition of the sitting justices

The supermajority rule was, at the time of its 2008 enactment, presented as a measure to ensure broad consensus on appointments. In practice, it has functioned as something closer to a veto power for the sitting Court over its successors — at least when the sitting justices vote as a bloc.

This is the structural feature that Cohen-Eliya and other reform-aligned scholars identify as the operating mechanism of what they call the “juristocracy”: the Court’s institutional capacity to perpetuate its own composition through the supermajority gate.

The seniority convention for Court President

By convention (not statute), the most senior sitting justice becomes the next President of the Supreme Court upon the prior President’s retirement. The Committee formally elects the President, but in practice the choice is determined by seniority.

This convention came under stress in 2023-2025 when Justice Minister Yariv Levin refused to convene the Committee for ~16 months after Esther Hayut’s October 2023 retirement, in order to block the elevation of Yitzhak Amit (next in seniority).

The High Court of Justice ultimately ordered the Committee convened, and Amit was elected President in January 2025 — with the coalition members boycotting both the vote and the subsequent swearing-in ceremony. See data/rulings/ for related documentary entries.

The March 2025 reconstitution law

On 27 March 2025, the Knesset passed a law restructuring the Committee’s composition:

  • The 2 Bar Association representatives were removed
  • 2 new positions were added: 1 lawyer chosen by the coalition, 1 lawyer chosen by the opposition

This shifts the balance toward political control of the Committee, though less aggressively than the original January 2023 Levin-Rothman proposal (which would have given the coalition automatic majority).

The new law takes effect at the start of the next Knesset — meaning it doesn’t apply to current Committee operations but will reshape future appointments.

Why this is the central post-2024 reform battleground

After the January 2024 Supreme Court ruling striking down the reasonableness amendment, the broader reform package was effectively dead. The coalition pivoted from direct constraint of the Court’s powers to changing who sits on the Court through Committee restructuring.

The logic is the same logic that Cohen-Eliya identifies in Israel’s Juristocracy: the Court’s authority and the Committee’s composition are mutually reinforcing. The Court’s authority over Knesset legislation depends on the Court’s composition; the Committee’s composition controls the Court’s composition; the supermajority rule gives the Court itself a veto over the Committee’s composition decisions.

Breaking this circuit, in the reform-aligned view, is necessary to restore democratic accountability to the appointment of judges. In the defenders’ view, breaking this circuit risks turning the Court into a partisan instrument and undermining judicial independence.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the existing supermajority structure — including Prof. Suzie Navot (Reichman / IDI), Prof. Mordechai Kremnitzer (IDI senior fellow), and the institutional position of the Israel Democracy Institute — argue:

  1. Judicial independence requires insulation from majoritarian politics. Most democracies that achieve judicial independence do so by removing senior judicial appointments from the immediate electoral cycle; the 2008 supermajority reform was precisely such an insulation mechanism, and reversing it would re-introduce the cyclical politicization Israel deliberately moved away from.
  2. Supermajority requirements ensure broad consensus across political shifts. A 7-of-9 threshold means a justice cannot be appointed (or blocked) on the strength of a thin coalition’s preferences; this stabilizes the Court’s composition against the unusually high turnover of Israeli governments.
  3. The “sitting-Court veto” framing overstates the empirical record. Defenders note that in the years since 2008, the Committee has appointed justices across the ideological spectrum, including appointees viewed as conservative (Sohlberg, Mintz, Elron). The supermajority gate has not, in practice, perpetuated a single ideological bloc.
  4. Removing the Bar reps (in the 2025 law) reduces professional input in favor of pure politics — the Bar’s institutional position is meant to weight the professional-competence consideration above ideological alignment, and political-only selection would erode that.
  5. Comparative-constitutional argument: most consolidated democracies use supermajority or counter-majoritarian mechanisms for senior judicial appointments. Israel’s 2008 model is structurally closer to the international consensus than to outlier majoritarian designs.

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Koppel, and Rothman, holds that: 1. Independence from politics is not the same as immunity from democratic legitimacy 2. The supermajority “consensus” requirement in practice gives the sitting Court an over-representative voice in its own succession 3. Bar Association representation gives outsized weight to a professional guild whose interests do not necessarily align with broader public preferences

This explainer documents the structure and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Wikipedia — Judicial Selection Committee (Israel)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — How Israeli Judges Are Appointed
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Ministers Levin and Sa’ar’s Proposed Changes to the Judicial Selection Committee
  • Lawfare — Israel’s Renewed Judicial Overhaul
  • Times of Israel — Judicial appointments law is Levin’s victory

explainers.title: The Reasonableness Doctrine (עילת הסבירות)
explainers.summary: What "reasonableness" means as a ground for judicial review of executive decisions, how the doctrine expanded under Aharon Barak, and why it became the central battleground of the 2023 reform.
explainers.summary_he: מהי 'סבירות' כעילה לביקורת שיפוטית על החלטות הרשות המבצעת, כיצד הורחבה הדוקטרינה תחת אהרן ברק, ומדוע הפכה לזירת ההתגוששות המרכזית של רפורמת 2023.
explainers.summary_en: What "reasonableness" means as a ground for judicial review of executive decisions, how the doctrine expanded under Aharon Barak, and why it became the central battleground of the 2023 reform.
explainers.body_html:
What it is

“Reasonableness” is a ground for judicial review of administrative and executive decisions. A court applying the reasonableness doctrine asks: did the decision-maker — a minister, a cabinet, an agency head — choose an option that any reasonable decision-maker could have chosen, given the facts and the law?

If the court answers “no,” the decision can be struck down.

In its original, narrow form, the doctrine was the common-law Wednesbury unreasonableness standard, formulated in the 1948 English ruling Associated Provincial Picture Houses v. Wednesbury Corporation. Under that standard, a court intervenes only when an administrative decision is “so outrageous in its defiance of logic or accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the question to be decided could have arrived at it.” This is a high threshold; intervention is rare.

How the doctrine expanded in Israel

Israeli administrative law adopted Wednesbury reasonableness from British common law in the early years of the state. Through the 1970s the standard was applied narrowly, in line with its British origin.

The transition into a standalone Israeli ground of review is generally traced to HCJ 156/75 Fawzi Dakka v. Minister of Transportation (1976), where Justice Meir Shamgar articulated unreasonableness as an independent basis for invalidating administrative action — not merely as evidence of some other defect:

“As a general matter it must first be established that statutory powers — especially those bearing on the rights of the individual — ought to be exercised reasonably. An authority that fails this duty acts outside the scope of its authority. From the general to the particular: a regulation infected by unreasonableness going to the root of the matter is regarded as exceeding its maker’s authority, and is therefore null and void.”

— Justice Shamgar, HCJ 156/75, פ״ד ל(2) 94 at 101 (public domain; Copyright Act 2007 §6). Hebrew original: “כללית יש להקדים ולקבוע כי סמכויות סטטוטוריות — ובעיקר אלה אשר להן נגיעה או זיקה לזכויות הפרט — מן הראוי שיופעלו באופן סביר. רשות הנמנעת מקיום חובה זו פועלת מחוץ לגדר סמכותה. מן הכלל לפרט: תקנה אשר דבקה בה האי־סבירות היורדת לשרשו של ענין, רואים אותה כחורגת מסמכותו של מי שהתקינה ובשל כך תיחשב כבטלה ומבוטלת.”

At this stage the threshold was still close to Wednesbury — unreasonableness “going to the root” of the matter. The decisive widening came later.

This changed substantially under the tenure of Justice Aharon Barak. Per Prof. Moshe Cohen-Eliya’s account in Israel’s Juristocracy (Law & Liberty, March 2024):

“Barak essentially abandoned this test in the 1980s and instead crafted the ‘balancing’ version of reasonableness, in which judges exercise discretionary executive powers if they conclude that the government did not correctly weigh or ‘balance’ different factors or considerations.”

The shift moved the doctrine from “is the decision crazy?” to “did the decision-maker properly balance the relevant considerations?” This is a fundamentally different question — and a substantially lower threshold for judicial intervention.

The expanded “balancing” doctrine became the most frequently used ground for striking down executive decisions in Israel.

Empirical scope (the “16 cases” claim)

Cohen-Eliya, in the same essay, notes the empirical scope of how often the doctrine was actually used at the level of ministerial and cabinet decisions:

“in the past decade the Supreme Court had intervened in only 16 ministerial or cabinet decisions due to their unreasonableness”

This figure is roughly corroborated by the Israel Democracy Institute, which writes that the Court has “struck down a mere 1.6 decisions a year based on this standard.”

The smaller-than-expected number is a key part of the debate: critics of the doctrine argue it has had outsized cultural and structural effects (administrative officials self-censor, knowing review is possible) even where actual strikedowns are rare. Defenders argue the small number shows the Court applies the doctrine with restraint.

Examples of decade-window reasonableness rulings against ministerial or cabinet decisions are documented in this project’s data/rulings/ directory — see 8948-22-deri.json, 5782-21-zilber.json, 1892-14-acri-prison-space.json, 3132-15-yesh-atid-litzman.json.

The 2023 amendment and the January 2024 strike-down

In July 2023, the Knesset enacted Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary, adding §15(d1): courts shall not review the reasonableness of decisions of the government, the prime minister, or any other minister.

The amendment did not abolish the reasonableness doctrine for administrative bodies generally — it removed it specifically for the most powerful tier of executive actors (cabinet, PM, ministers).

In January 2024, the Supreme Court, sitting en banc as a 15-justice panel, struck down the amendment in HCJ 5658/23 by an 8-7 majority. The Court additionally held, by a separate 12-3 ruling, that it has authority in principle to review Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority. See data/rulings/5658-23.json for the full structured record.

Why this is the central battleground

The reasonableness doctrine sits at the intersection of two fundamental questions about how Israeli democracy works:

  1. Who decides what executive officials can do? The legislature (which writes the laws and confirms ministers) or the judiciary (which reviews the resulting decisions)?

  2. What is the threshold for judicial intervention? A clearly-defined legal violation (high threshold, narrow scope) or the judge’s view of whether the decision was properly balanced (lower threshold, much broader scope)?

The doctrinal expansion under Barak shifted the answers to both questions: judges decide what ministers can do; the threshold is a judicial assessment of balance rather than a clear legal violation.

The 2023 reform sought to reverse this, at least at the cabinet/PM/minister level. The 2024 Supreme Court ruling reversed the reversal.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the expanded reasonableness doctrine — among them Justice Daphne Barak-Erez (a sitting member of the Court who has written academically on judicial review before her appointment), Prof. Aeyal Gross (Tel Aviv University), and Prof. Suzie Navot (Reichman / IDI) — advance several arguments worth engaging directly. Gross’s An Unreasonable Amendment: The Constitutional Capture in Israel (Verfassungsblog, July 2023) is one of the more developed academic defenses of the doctrine against the 2023 reform bill.

The structural argument: Israel lacks the institutional checks present in other democracies — no second legislative chamber, no federalism, no entrenched written constitution — and so the Court’s review role is structurally necessary as the “only real check” (a phrase used in the HCJ 5658/23 majority opinion). Under this account, dismantling reasonableness leaves no operative restraint on cabinet decisions between elections.

The empirical-restraint argument: per the Israel Democracy Institute, the Court has struck down approximately 1.6 ministerial or cabinet decisions per year on reasonableness grounds — the same figure Cohen-Eliya cites as “16 cases” in the decade. The defenders read this number the opposite way: not as evidence of outsized cultural effect, but as evidence of judicial restraint within a doctrine that could be used much more broadly. The cultural-deterrence claim, on this reading, attributes effects to the doctrine that ordinary democratic accountability would produce anyway.

The gap-filling argument: reasonableness covers cases — particularly around appointments, discretionary administrative actions, and decisions touching minority rights — that other doctrines (proportionality, ultra vires) do not reach. Removing it without replacement creates immunity zones the prior law explicitly avoided.

The case-law-incrementalism argument: the “balancing” form of reasonableness developed case-by-case over four decades through ordinary judicial reasoning, not by single act. Defenders treat the doctrine’s elasticity as the normal evolution of common-law review, not as overreach.

The counter-position — articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Koppel, and others — is that the expansion has no parallel in any other Western democracy; that it amounts to judicial substitution of judgment for the elected executive’s; and that the doctrine’s “balancing” form is so elastic that it cannot be defined or constrained in advance.

This explainer documents the doctrine and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy,” Law & Liberty (March 2024)
  • Israel Democracy Institute, “The Supreme Court Ruling on Canceling the Reasonableness Clause — Implications”
  • Israel Democracy Institute, “Doing Away with the Standard of Extreme Unreasonableness”
  • INSS, “The Supreme Court on the Reasonableness Standard”
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat, “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)
explainers.body_html_he:
מהי

“סבירות” היא עילה לביקורת שיפוטית על החלטות מנהליות והחלטות של הרשות המבצעת. בית משפט המפעיל את עילת הסבירות שואל: האם מקבל ההחלטה — שר, ממשלה או ראש רשות — בחר באפשרות שכל מקבל החלטה סביר היה יכול לבחור בה, בהינתן העובדות והדין?

אם בית המשפט משיב “לא”, ניתן לבטל את ההחלטה.

בצורתה המקורית והמצומצמת, הדוקטרינה הייתה מבחן חוסר הסבירות מסוג Wednesbury של המשפט המקובל, שגובש בפסק הדין האנגלי משנת 1948 Associated Provincial Picture Houses v. Wednesbury Corporation. על פי מבחן זה, בית המשפט מתערב רק כאשר החלטה מנהלית היא “כה מקוממת בהתעלמותה מן ההיגיון או מאמות מידה מוסריות מקובלות, עד שאף אדם שפוי שהפעיל את שיקול דעתו על השאלה שעמדה להכרעה לא היה יכול להגיע אליה”. זהו רף גבוה; ההתערבות נדירה.

כיצד הורחבה הדוקטרינה בישראל

המשפט המנהלי הישראלי אימץ את סבירות Wednesbury מן המשפט המקובל הבריטי בשנותיה הראשונות של המדינה. במהלך שנות השבעים יושם המבחן באופן מצומצם, בהתאם למקורו הבריטי.

את המעבר לעילה ישראלית עצמאית נהוג לייחס לבג״ץ 156/75 פאוזי דקה נ׳ שר התחבורה (1976), שבו ניסח השופט מאיר שמגר את אי-הסבירות כעילה עצמאית לביטול פעולה מנהלית — ולא רק כראיה לפגם אחר. בלשונו (פ״ד ל(2) 94, בעמ’ 101):

“כללית יש להקדים ולקבוע כי סמכויות סטטוטוריות — ובעיקר אלה אשר להן נגיעה או זיקה לזכויות הפרט — מן הראוי שיופעלו באופן סביר. רשות הנמנעת מקיום חובה זו פועלת מחוץ לגדר סמכותה. מן הכלל לפרט: תקנה אשר דבקה בה האי־סבירות היורדת לשרשו של ענין, רואים אותה כחורגת מסמכותו של מי שהתקינה ובשל כך תיחשב כבטלה ומבוטלת.”

(הועתק מן הפסק הרשמי, כרך ל(2) עמ’ 101 — נחלת הכלל לפי סעיף 6 לחוק זכות יוצרים, התשס״ז-2007.)

בשלב זה הרף עדיין נותר קרוב למבחן Wednesbury — אי־סבירות “היורדת לשרשו של ענין”. ההרחבה המכרעת הגיעה מאוחר יותר.

מצב זה השתנה באופן מהותי בתקופת כהונתו של השופט אהרן ברק. כך מתאר זאת פרופ’ משה כהן-אליה בIsrael’s Juristocracy (Law & Liberty, מרץ 2024):

“ברק זנח למעשה מבחן זה בשנות השמונים, ובמקומו עיצב את גרסת ה’איזון’ של הסבירות, שבה השופטים מפעילים שיקול דעת בעניין סמכויות ביצועיות אם הם מסיקים שהממשלה לא שקלה כראוי או לא ‘איזנה’ נכון בין גורמים ושיקולים שונים.”

המעבר העתיק את הדוקטרינה משאלת “האם ההחלטה מטורפת?” לשאלת “האם מקבל ההחלטה איזן כראוי בין השיקולים הרלוונטיים?”. זוהי שאלה שונה באופן יסודי — ורף נמוך בהרבה להתערבות שיפוטית.

דוקטרינת ה”איזון” המורחבת הפכה לעילה הנפוצה ביותר לביטול החלטות של הרשות המבצעת בישראל.

ההיקף האמפירי (טענת “16 המקרים”)

כהן-אליה, באותו מאמר, מציין את ההיקף האמפירי של תדירות השימוש בפועל בדוקטרינה ברמת החלטות של שרים ושל הממשלה:

“בעשור האחרון התערב בית המשפט העליון ב-16 החלטות בלבד של שרים או של הממשלה בשל חוסר סבירותן”

נתון זה נתמך בקירוב על ידי המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה, המציין כי בית המשפט “פסל בממוצע 1.6 החלטות בלבד בשנה על בסיס מבחן זה”.

המספר הנמוך מהצפוי הוא חלק מרכזי מהמחלוקת: מבקרי הדוקטרינה טוענים כי היו לה השפעות תרבותיות ומבניות חורגות מן הפרופורציה (פקידי מנהל מצנזרים את עצמם, ביודעם שהביקורת אפשרית) גם כשהפסילות בפועל נדירות. מצדדיה טוענים כי המספר הקטן מלמד שבית המשפט מפעיל את הדוקטרינה בריסון.

דוגמאות לפסיקות סבירות בחלון העשור כנגד החלטות של שרים או של הממשלה מתועדות בספריית data/rulings/ של פרויקט זה — ראו 8948-22-deri.json, 5782-21-zilber.json, 1892-14-acri-prison-space.json, 3132-15-yesh-atid-litzman.json.

תיקון 2023 והפסילה של ינואר 2024

ביולי 2023 חוקקה הכנסת את תיקון מס’ 3 לחוק-יסוד: השפיטה, שהוסיף את סעיף 15(ד1): בתי המשפט לא יידונו בסבירות החלטותיהם של הממשלה, ראש הממשלה או כל שר אחר.

התיקון לא ביטל את עילת הסבירות לגבי גופים מנהליים בכלל — הוא ביטל אותה באופן ספציפי לגבי הדרג החזק ביותר של הרשות המבצעת (הממשלה, ראש הממשלה והשרים).

בינואר 2024 פסל בית המשפט העליון, בשבתו בהרכב מלא של 15 שופטים, את התיקון בבג”ץ 5658/23 ברוב של 8 מול 7. בית המשפט קבע בנוסף, ברוב נפרד של 12 מול 3, כי יש לו סמכות עקרונית לבקר חוקי-יסוד החורגים מסמכותה המכוננת של הכנסת. ראו data/rulings/5658-23.json לרשומה המובנית המלאה.

מדוע זוהי זירת ההתגוששות המרכזית

עילת הסבירות ניצבת בנקודת המפגש של שתי שאלות יסוד באשר לאופן פעולתה של הדמוקרטיה הישראלית:

  1. מי מכריע מה רשאים נושאי משרה ברשות המבצעת לעשות? הרשות המחוקקת (המחוקקת את החוקים ומאשרת את השרים) או הרשות השופטת (המבקרת את ההחלטות הנובעות מכך)?

  2. מהו הרף להתערבות שיפוטית? הפרה משפטית מוגדרת בבירור (רף גבוה, היקף מצומצם) או עמדת השופט באשר לשאלה אם ההחלטה אוזנה כראוי (רף נמוך, היקף רחב בהרבה)?

ההרחבה הדוקטרינרית בתקופת ברק שינתה את התשובות לשתי השאלות: השופטים מכריעים מה רשאים השרים לעשות; הרף הוא הערכה שיפוטית של איזון ולא הפרה משפטית ברורה.

רפורמת 2023 ביקשה להפוך מגמה זו, לפחות ברמת הממשלה/ראש הממשלה/השרים. פסיקת בית המשפט העליון מ-2024 הפכה את ההיפוך.

הערה על הוגנות אדוורסרית

מצדדי עילת הסבירות המורחבת — ובהם השופטת דפנה ברק-ארז (חברה מכהנת בבית המשפט, שכתבה אקדמית על ביקורת שיפוטית טרם מינויה), פרופ’ אייל גרוס (אוניברסיטת תל אביב) ופרופ’ סוזי נבות (רייכמן / המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה) — מעלים מספר טענות שראוי להתמודד עמן ישירות. מאמרו של גרוס, An Unreasonable Amendment: The Constitutional Capture in Israel (Verfassungsblog, יולי 2023), הוא אחד ההגנות האקדמיות המפותחות יותר על הדוקטרינה אל מול הצעת חוק הרפורמה של 2023.

הטיעון המבני: בישראל חסרים האיזונים המוסדיים הקיימים בדמוקרטיות אחרות — אין בית מחוקקים שני, אין פדרליזם, אין חוקה כתובה ומשוריינת — ועל כן תפקיד הביקורת של בית המשפט הכרחי מבחינה מבנית כ”בלם האמיתי היחיד” (ביטוי שהופיע בדעת הרוב בבג”ץ 5658/23). על פי גישה זו, פירוק עילת הסבירות מותיר את החלטות הממשלה ללא ריסון אופרטיבי בין מערכות בחירות.

טיעון הריסון האמפירי: על פי המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה, בית המשפט פסל כ-1.6 החלטות של שרים או של הממשלה בשנה מטעמי סבירות — אותו נתון שכהן-אליה מצטט כ”16 מקרים” בעשור. המצדדים קוראים מספר זה באופן הפוך: לא כעדות להשפעה תרבותית חורגת מהפרופורציה, אלא כעדות לריסון שיפוטי בתוך דוקטרינה שיכלה לשמש באופן רחב בהרבה. טענת ההרתעה התרבותית, על פי קריאה זו, מייחסת לדוקטרינה השפעות שאחריותיות דמוקרטית רגילה הייתה מייצרת ממילא.

טיעון מילוי החסר: עילת הסבירות חולשת על מקרים — בייחוד סביב מינויים, פעולות מנהליות שבשיקול דעת, והחלטות הנוגעות בזכויות מיעוטים — שדוקטרינות אחרות (מידתיות, חריגה מסמכות) אינן מגיעות אליהם. הסרתה ללא תחליף יוצרת אזורי חסינות שהדין הקודם נמנע מהם במפורש.

טיעון ההתפתחות ההדרגתית בפסיקה: צורת ה”איזון” של הסבירות התפתחה מקרה אחר מקרה לאורך ארבעה עשורים באמצעות הנמקה שיפוטית רגילה, ולא בפעולה יחידה. המצדדים רואים בגמישות הדוקטרינה את ההתפתחות הרגילה של ביקורת במשפט המקובל, ולא חריגה מסמכות.

העמדה הנגדית — כפי שמנוסחת על ידי כהן-אליה, קופל ואחרים — היא שלהרחבה אין מקבילה באף דמוקרטיה מערבית אחרת; שהיא מהווה החלפת שיקול הדעת של הרשות המבצעת הנבחרת בשיקול דעת שיפוטי; ושצורת ה”איזון” של הדוקטרינה כה גמישה עד שלא ניתן להגדירה או לתחום אותה מראש.

מאמר הסבר זה מתעד את הדוקטרינה ואת המחלוקת; הוא אינו מכריע מי משתי העמדות צודקת.

קריאה נוספת

  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy,” Law & Liberty (מרץ 2024)
  • המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה, “פסיקת בית המשפט העליון על ביטול עילת הסבירות — השלכות”
  • המכון הישראלי לדמוקרטיה, “ביטול מבחן חוסר הסבירות הקיצוני”
  • INSS, “בית המשפט העליון ומבחן הסבירות”
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat, “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)
explainers.body_html_en:
What it is

“Reasonableness” is a ground for judicial review of administrative and executive decisions. A court applying the reasonableness doctrine asks: did the decision-maker — a minister, a cabinet, an agency head — choose an option that any reasonable decision-maker could have chosen, given the facts and the law?

If the court answers “no,” the decision can be struck down.

In its original, narrow form, the doctrine was the common-law Wednesbury unreasonableness standard, formulated in the 1948 English ruling Associated Provincial Picture Houses v. Wednesbury Corporation. Under that standard, a court intervenes only when an administrative decision is “so outrageous in its defiance of logic or accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the question to be decided could have arrived at it.” This is a high threshold; intervention is rare.

How the doctrine expanded in Israel

Israeli administrative law adopted Wednesbury reasonableness from British common law in the early years of the state. Through the 1970s the standard was applied narrowly, in line with its British origin.

The transition into a standalone Israeli ground of review is generally traced to HCJ 156/75 Fawzi Dakka v. Minister of Transportation (1976), where Justice Meir Shamgar articulated unreasonableness as an independent basis for invalidating administrative action — not merely as evidence of some other defect:

“As a general matter it must first be established that statutory powers — especially those bearing on the rights of the individual — ought to be exercised reasonably. An authority that fails this duty acts outside the scope of its authority. From the general to the particular: a regulation infected by unreasonableness going to the root of the matter is regarded as exceeding its maker’s authority, and is therefore null and void.”

— Justice Shamgar, HCJ 156/75, פ״ד ל(2) 94 at 101 (public domain; Copyright Act 2007 §6). Hebrew original: “כללית יש להקדים ולקבוע כי סמכויות סטטוטוריות — ובעיקר אלה אשר להן נגיעה או זיקה לזכויות הפרט — מן הראוי שיופעלו באופן סביר. רשות הנמנעת מקיום חובה זו פועלת מחוץ לגדר סמכותה. מן הכלל לפרט: תקנה אשר דבקה בה האי־סבירות היורדת לשרשו של ענין, רואים אותה כחורגת מסמכותו של מי שהתקינה ובשל כך תיחשב כבטלה ומבוטלת.”

At this stage the threshold was still close to Wednesbury — unreasonableness “going to the root” of the matter. The decisive widening came later.

This changed substantially under the tenure of Justice Aharon Barak. Per Prof. Moshe Cohen-Eliya’s account in Israel’s Juristocracy (Law & Liberty, March 2024):

“Barak essentially abandoned this test in the 1980s and instead crafted the ‘balancing’ version of reasonableness, in which judges exercise discretionary executive powers if they conclude that the government did not correctly weigh or ‘balance’ different factors or considerations.”

The shift moved the doctrine from “is the decision crazy?” to “did the decision-maker properly balance the relevant considerations?” This is a fundamentally different question — and a substantially lower threshold for judicial intervention.

The expanded “balancing” doctrine became the most frequently used ground for striking down executive decisions in Israel.

Empirical scope (the “16 cases” claim)

Cohen-Eliya, in the same essay, notes the empirical scope of how often the doctrine was actually used at the level of ministerial and cabinet decisions:

“in the past decade the Supreme Court had intervened in only 16 ministerial or cabinet decisions due to their unreasonableness”

This figure is roughly corroborated by the Israel Democracy Institute, which writes that the Court has “struck down a mere 1.6 decisions a year based on this standard.”

The smaller-than-expected number is a key part of the debate: critics of the doctrine argue it has had outsized cultural and structural effects (administrative officials self-censor, knowing review is possible) even where actual strikedowns are rare. Defenders argue the small number shows the Court applies the doctrine with restraint.

Examples of decade-window reasonableness rulings against ministerial or cabinet decisions are documented in this project’s data/rulings/ directory — see 8948-22-deri.json, 5782-21-zilber.json, 1892-14-acri-prison-space.json, 3132-15-yesh-atid-litzman.json.

The 2023 amendment and the January 2024 strike-down

In July 2023, the Knesset enacted Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary, adding §15(d1): courts shall not review the reasonableness of decisions of the government, the prime minister, or any other minister.

The amendment did not abolish the reasonableness doctrine for administrative bodies generally — it removed it specifically for the most powerful tier of executive actors (cabinet, PM, ministers).

In January 2024, the Supreme Court, sitting en banc as a 15-justice panel, struck down the amendment in HCJ 5658/23 by an 8-7 majority. The Court additionally held, by a separate 12-3 ruling, that it has authority in principle to review Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority. See data/rulings/5658-23.json for the full structured record.

Why this is the central battleground

The reasonableness doctrine sits at the intersection of two fundamental questions about how Israeli democracy works:

  1. Who decides what executive officials can do? The legislature (which writes the laws and confirms ministers) or the judiciary (which reviews the resulting decisions)?

  2. What is the threshold for judicial intervention? A clearly-defined legal violation (high threshold, narrow scope) or the judge’s view of whether the decision was properly balanced (lower threshold, much broader scope)?

The doctrinal expansion under Barak shifted the answers to both questions: judges decide what ministers can do; the threshold is a judicial assessment of balance rather than a clear legal violation.

The 2023 reform sought to reverse this, at least at the cabinet/PM/minister level. The 2024 Supreme Court ruling reversed the reversal.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the expanded reasonableness doctrine — among them Justice Daphne Barak-Erez (a sitting member of the Court who has written academically on judicial review before her appointment), Prof. Aeyal Gross (Tel Aviv University), and Prof. Suzie Navot (Reichman / IDI) — advance several arguments worth engaging directly. Gross’s An Unreasonable Amendment: The Constitutional Capture in Israel (Verfassungsblog, July 2023) is one of the more developed academic defenses of the doctrine against the 2023 reform bill.

The structural argument: Israel lacks the institutional checks present in other democracies — no second legislative chamber, no federalism, no entrenched written constitution — and so the Court’s review role is structurally necessary as the “only real check” (a phrase used in the HCJ 5658/23 majority opinion). Under this account, dismantling reasonableness leaves no operative restraint on cabinet decisions between elections.

The empirical-restraint argument: per the Israel Democracy Institute, the Court has struck down approximately 1.6 ministerial or cabinet decisions per year on reasonableness grounds — the same figure Cohen-Eliya cites as “16 cases” in the decade. The defenders read this number the opposite way: not as evidence of outsized cultural effect, but as evidence of judicial restraint within a doctrine that could be used much more broadly. The cultural-deterrence claim, on this reading, attributes effects to the doctrine that ordinary democratic accountability would produce anyway.

The gap-filling argument: reasonableness covers cases — particularly around appointments, discretionary administrative actions, and decisions touching minority rights — that other doctrines (proportionality, ultra vires) do not reach. Removing it without replacement creates immunity zones the prior law explicitly avoided.

The case-law-incrementalism argument: the “balancing” form of reasonableness developed case-by-case over four decades through ordinary judicial reasoning, not by single act. Defenders treat the doctrine’s elasticity as the normal evolution of common-law review, not as overreach.

The counter-position — articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Koppel, and others — is that the expansion has no parallel in any other Western democracy; that it amounts to judicial substitution of judgment for the elected executive’s; and that the doctrine’s “balancing” form is so elastic that it cannot be defined or constrained in advance.

This explainer documents the doctrine and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy,” Law & Liberty (March 2024)
  • Israel Democracy Institute, “The Supreme Court Ruling on Canceling the Reasonableness Clause — Implications”
  • Israel Democracy Institute, “Doing Away with the Standard of Extreme Unreasonableness”
  • INSS, “The Supreme Court on the Reasonableness Standard”
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat, “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)

The data spans nearly five decades and three doctrinal layers — foundational power-assertion (1976-1995), abuse-of-constituent-power crystallization (2021-2024), and active intervention (2014-2026). 12 of 13 cases either struck the challenged measure or asserted new Court authority over it; 1 declined.

patterns.body_html:
Pattern: Israeli Supreme Court Interventions in Elected-Branch Decisions, 1976-2026

This document is a structured pattern analysis drawn from the documentary core of this project. It surveys 13 landmark Supreme Court rulings — selected from the project’s documentary core (21 rulings in total) as the cases that trace the power-acquisition arc — and identifies recurring features. For the complete catalog, see the rulings index.

This is empirical: every quantitative claim below cites the specific case-IDs in the documentary core. Readers can verify each count by examining the JSON entries directly.

The documentary core, summarized

The 13 selected landmark rulings, in chronological order:

# Case Date Disposition Doctrine Petitioner
1 Dakka v. Min. Transportation 1976 Declarative (denied; doctrine established) Reasonableness (foundation) Individual
2 Bank Mizrahi v. Migdal 1995-11-09 Declarative (statute upheld; judicial-review power asserted) Constitutional supremacy Individual (commercial)
3 Sela v. Yehieli (Kfar Vradim mikveh) 2014-09-09 Struck down Reasonableness Individuals
4 Yesh Atid v. PM (Litzman) 2015-08-23 Struck down Reasonableness; Basic Law: Government Party
5 ACRI v. Min. Public Security 2017-06-13 Struck down (mandatory order) Reasonableness NGO
6 Shafir v. Knesset 2021-05-23 Warning of voidness Abuse of constituent power Party
7 Zilber v. Min. Finance 2022-01-12 Partially struck Reasonableness Individual
8 Deri (HCJ 8948/22) 2023-01-18 Struck down Reasonableness NGO
9 HCJ 5658/23 (reasonableness amendment) 2024-01-01 Struck down Constituent authority limits; Basic Law: Judiciary NGO
10 HCJ 5119/23 (incapacitation law) 2024-01-03 Partially struck Abuse of constituent power NGO
11 MQG v. Min. Justice (Levin / Judicial Selection Committee) 2024-09-12 Mandatory order Ultra vires; separation of powers NGO
12 Baharav-Miara dismissal 2025-12-14 Struck down Procedural review; conflict of interest NGO
13 Zini / Shin Bet 2025-12-28 Dismissed (Court declined to intervene) Conflict of interest; reasonableness NGO

Pattern 1: Direction of disposition

Grouped by the Court’s action, these 13 rulings break down as:

Category Count Cases
Active intervention (struck / partially struck / remanded / mandatory order) 9 Sela, Litzman, ACRI, Zilber, Deri, HCJ 5658/23, HCJ 5119/23, Levin/JSC, Baharav-Miara
Doctrinal power-assertion without immediate strike (declarative or warning-of-voidness) 3 Dakka 1976 (reasonableness established), Bank Mizrahi 1995 (constitutional-supremacy + judicial-review-of-legislation asserted), Shafir 2021 (abuse-of-constituent-power test articulated)
Court declined to intervene (dismissed) 1 Zini

Out of 13 rulings, 12 either struck the challenged measure or asserted new Court authority over its class of measures. The single exception (Zini) was itself contested within the Court — 2-1, with the current President Amit dissenting.

Caveat on this pattern: The documentary core is a non-random selection of high-profile rulings, not a representative sample. The Court rules on thousands of petitions per year, most of which never produce the kind of intervention documented here. What the 13:1 ratio shows is not the Court’s overall intervention rate; it shows the shape of high-profile cases that reach the documentary surface — that when the Court engages with an elected-branch decision of consequence, asserting authority over it (whether by striking, compelling, or expanding doctrine) is the default outcome.

Pattern 2: Three doctrinal layers

The dataset spans 50 years across three identifiable doctrinal layers:

Layer A — Foundational power-assertion (1976-1995)

Case What was asserted
Dakka 1976 Reasonableness as standalone ground of administrative review (later expanded into the “balancing” form that drives most of Layer C)
Bank Mizrahi 1995 Basic Laws as constitutional rank; judicial power to invalidate primary legislation that conflicts with them — both asserted in obiter dicta in a private commercial-banking appeal

In neither case did the immediate disposition strike anything. The doctrinal architecture asserted in these rulings is what makes Layers B and C possible.

Layer B — Abuse-of-constituent-power crystallization (2021-2024)

Case Step in the doctrinal arc
Shafir 2021 Tripartite test established (abuse of constituent power, generality, compatibility with constitutional fabric); warning of voidness issued
HCJ 5658/23 (Jan 2024) First operative use to strike a Basic Law amendment — the reasonableness amendment, 8-7 en banc
HCJ 5119/23 (Jan 2024) Second operative use, same day; the incapacitation amendment held to apply only from next Knesset (6-5 partial strike via delayed-effect interpretation)

Layer B is the project’s most concentrated doctrinal cluster: a single test articulated in Shafir was used twice on the same day in January 2024 against the most consequential Basic Law amendments the Knesset had passed in that legislative cycle.

Layer C — Active intervention in elected-branch decisions (2014-2026)

The other 8 cases in the dataset: Sela, Litzman, ACRI, Zilber, Deri, Levin/JSC, Baharav-Miara, Zini. These apply doctrines from Layers A and B to specific government decisions and appointments.

Pattern 3: Doctrinal distribution

Doctrines invoked across the 13 rulings:

Doctrine Count Cases
Reasonableness 7 Dakka (foundation), Sela, Litzman, ACRI, Zilber, Deri, Levin/JSC
Abuse of constituent power 3 Shafir (foundation), HCJ 5658/23, HCJ 5119/23
Constituent authority limits 4 Shafir, HCJ 5658/23, HCJ 5119/23, Bank Mizrahi (obliquely)
Procedural review 1 Baharav-Miara
Conflict of interest 2 Baharav-Miara, Zini
Basic Law: Government 2 Litzman, Shafir
Basic Law: Judiciary 1 HCJ 5658/23
Constitutional supremacy 1 Bank Mizrahi
Ultra vires 1 Levin/JSC
Separation of powers 1 Levin/JSC
Judicial independence 1 Levin/JSC

Reasonableness remains the most-used doctrine — invoked in 7 of 13 cases, spanning from its 1976 foundation in Dakka through the 2024 Levin/JSC mandatory order. The 2024 reasonableness amendment (struck in HCJ 5658/23) would have eliminated reasonableness review for cabinet/PM/minister decisions. Had it stood, four of the rulings in this documentary core (Litzman, Deri, Zilber, Levin/JSC) might not have proceeded on their current grounds. The Court’s decision to strike that amendment — preserving its most-used doctrine — is itself a documented pattern.

Abuse of constituent power is the second-most-used doctrinal cluster post-2021 — 3 of 13 cases in 5 years. All three cluster in the Shafir → HCJ 5658/23 → HCJ 5119/23 arc.

Pattern 4: Petitioner type

Who brings the petitions:

Petitioner type Count Cases
NGO 7 ACRI (#5), Deri (#8, MQG), HCJ 5658/23 (#9, MQG + Bar Assn), HCJ 5119/23 (#10, MQG), Levin/JSC (#11, MQG), Baharav-Miara (#12, MQG + Bar + others), Zini (#13, MQG + 4 ex-Shin Bet chiefs)
Individual 4 Dakka (#1), Bank Mizrahi (#2, commercial), Sela (#3), Zilber (#7)
Party 2 Litzman (#4, Yesh Atid), Shafir (#6, former MK + NGOs)

The Movement for Quality Government (התנועה למען איכות השלטון) appears as the lead or major co-petitioner in 6 of 13 cases (#8, #9, #10, #11, #12, #13) — Deri, HCJ 5658/23, HCJ 5119/23, Levin/JSC, Baharav-Miara, and Zini.

This concentration is structurally significant: a single repeat-player NGO drives the petition flow on the cases that most directly contest the elected branch’s appointment and Basic-Law-amendment authority. This is public information — MQG’s role is openly stated — but the empirical density of its presence in the post-2022 cluster (5 of 7 post-2022 cases in this dataset, or 71%) is itself a pattern.

Pattern 5: Time distribution

Rulings by period:

Period Count Cases
1976 1 Dakka (reasonableness foundation)
1995 1 Bank Mizrahi (judicial-review foundation)
1996-2013 0 —
2014-2017 3 Sela 2014; Litzman 2015; ACRI 2017
2018-2020 0 —
2021-2022 2 Shafir 2021; Zilber 2022
2023-2025 6 Deri 2023; HCJ 5658/23 Jan 2024; HCJ 5119/23 Jan 2024; Levin/JSC Sep 2024; Baharav-Miara Dec 2025; Zini Dec 2025

Nearly half of all 13 documented rulings — 6 of 13 — come from the 2023-2025 window (roughly three years). This includes two same-day Basic-Law-amendment rulings (5658/23 and 5119/23 on January 1-3, 2024), a mandatory-order against a sitting minister (Levin/JSC), the dismissal of an AG (Baharav-Miara), and a security-service-appointment case the Court declined to intervene on (Zini).

The blank stretches (1996-2013, 2018-2020) likely reflect the project’s selection focus on high-profile rulings and the political composition of those years’ coalitions; they are not claims that the Court did not intervene during those periods.

What the time distribution does show: the post-2022 period is unusually dense with documented interventions, and the doctrinal arc cataloged here (Layer A → Layer B → Layer C) reaches its operational peak in this period.

Pattern 6: What the elected branch was attempting

Decision types challenged in these 13 rulings:

Decision type Cases Count
Ministerial appointment Litzman, Deri, Zini 3
Basic Law amendment HCJ 5658/23, HCJ 5119/23, Shafir 3
Primary legislation (statute) Bank Mizrahi 1
AG dismissal Baharav-Miara 1
Ministerial inaction (failure to convene Selection Committee) Levin/JSC 1
Policy / regulation Zilber daycare cancellation 1
Administrative action / inaction ACRI prison-space 1
Administrative reasonableness (transportation) Dakka 1
Local-authority decision Sela mikveh 1

Appointments are the most-challenged decision type — 3 of 13 cases. Adding the AG-dismissal and the Selection-Committee inaction case, 5 of 13 cases concern judicial-or-AG-related personnel decisions — the structural mechanisms by which the legal-establishment composition is renewed.

Basic Law amendments are the second-most-challenged decision type — 3 of 13, all concentrated in the Layer B cluster (Shafir → 5658/23 → 5119/23). This is novel: prior to Shafir 2021, the Court had never operatively voided or warned-of-voidness a Basic Law amendment. Three such rulings in 32 months.

What the data shows together

Taking these patterns together, the documentary core supports several observations:

  1. The Court’s authority is self-asserted in obiter and operationalized later. The foundational power-assertions (Dakka 1976, Bank Mizrahi 1995, Shafir 2021) did not strike anything; they articulated tests later used to strike. Each layer was built before it was used.

  2. The active-intervention default is robust. In 12 of 13 documented cases the Court either struck the challenged measure or asserted new authority over its class. The single dismissal (Zini) was internally contested 2-1.

  3. Reasonableness is the operational core, and the Court protected it. Reasonableness appears in 7 of 13 cases across all three layers. The 2024 strike of the reasonableness amendment preserved the Court’s most-used doctrine.

  4. A small set of repeat NGOs drives the high-profile petitions. MQG appears in 6 of 13 cases (all six of the post-2022 rulings); the Israeli Bar Association is a frequent co-petitioner. The pattern is a small number of repeat-player petitioners producing a disproportionate share of the cases that reach this dataset.

  5. Appointments and structural-personnel decisions are the dominant contested category. Counting ministerial appointments (3) + AG dismissal (1) + Selection-Committee inaction (1) yields 5 of 13 — the cases that contest the renewal mechanism of the legal establishment specifically.

  6. The doctrinal expansion of Basic-Law-amendment review is concentrated in a narrow window. Three operative rulings in 32 months (Shafir May 2021; HCJ 5658/23 and 5119/23 same-day in January 2024).

What the data does NOT show

This pattern document is deliberately limited to what the documentary core empirically demonstrates. It does not, and is not intended to, support claims about:

  • The motives or political alignment of individual justices
  • Whether any specific intervention was substantively correct or incorrect
  • Whether the overall pattern of intervention is good or bad for Israeli democracy
  • The intentions of any petitioner organization

These questions are legitimate and are debated extensively elsewhere — including in the further-reading sources cited below. But they are not what the structured data can answer. The structured data answers: how often, on what grounds, brought by whom, with what outcome.

Further reading

  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy” — the framing this project’s editorial layer engages
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Doing Away with the Standard of Extreme Unreasonableness — the IDI’s empirical analysis
  • Ran Hirschl, Towards Juristocracy — the comparative-empirical theoretical anchor; Hirschl’s framework is critical-empirical rather than reform-prescriptive (and Hirschl himself, who died May 19 2026, did not endorse the 2023-2024 Israeli reform effort)
  • Verfassungsblog — Saving the Constitution from Politics (on Shafir)
  • See also the explainers in ../explainers/ (reasonableness doctrine, constituent authority, judicial selection committee) and the historical essay ../essays/from-bank-mizrahi-to-hcj-5658-23.md
