AAA 3782/12 (עע”מ 3782/12) — Tel Aviv-Jaffa District Commander v. Israel Internet Association (2013-03-24)

בוטל · הרכב: Justice Uzi Vogelman (majority opinion); President Asher Grunis (concurring); Justice Noam Sohlberg (dissenting)

המשטרה ניכסה לעצמה סמכות לצוות על ספקיות האינטרנט לחסום אתרי הימורים, בהישען על סמכות סגירת “מקום” לפי סעיף 229 לחוק העונשין, התשל”ז-1977; בית המשפט העליון דחה את ערעור המדינה וקבע — אף שהכיר בכך שאתר עשוי להיחשב “מקום” וחסימה כ”סגירה” — שאין כל הסמכה מפורשת לגייס צד שלישי פרטי לאכיפה, ובכך פסל כסמכות שלא הייתה וגזר על המשטרה לחזור לכנסת לחקיקה ראשית. שופט אחד (סולברג) חלק וסבר שהסמכות קיימת.

החוק: Penal Law, 5737-1977 (esp. sec. 229 — power to close ‘premises’ used for gambling); the police practice of ordering ISPs to block access to online gambling sites

מקור: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 7385/13 — Israeli Immigration Policy Center v. Government of Israel — HCJ 7385/13 (2014)

בוטל חלקית

תיקון מס’ 4 לחוק למניעת הסתננות (התשע”ד-2013) הקים מנגנון להרתעת מבקשי מקלט: מעצר סגור לשנה ושהייה בלתי-מוגבלת ב”חולות”. בית המשפט (הנשיאה נאור) פסל את שני העמודים — סעיף 30א וכל פרק “חולות” — והשעה את הבטלות ל-90 יום כדי שהכנסת תפרק את ההסדר. שופטים בלתי-נבחרים ביטלו את נסיון החקיקה השני של המחוקק הנבחר.

החוק: Prevention of Infiltration (Offences and Jurisdiction) Law, 5714-1954, as amended by Amendment No. 4 (5774-2013) — detention/residency-center provisions (notably section 30A and the ‘Holot’ open-facility chapter).

מקור: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 8425/13 (consolidated with בג”ץ 7385/13) / HCJ 8425/13 — Israeli Immigration Policy Center v. The Government (2014)

בוטל חלקית

חוק ועדות הקבלה (תיקון מס’ 8 לפקודת האגודות השיתופיות, 2011), המתיר ליישובים קטנים בנגב ובגליל לסנן מועמדים לפי “התאמה חברתית-תרבותית” — נותר על כנו במלואו. ברוב של 5 מול 4 דחו השופטים את העתירות בטענת חוסר בשלות, בלי לפסול ובלי לצמצם; ארבעה ביקשו לבטל את החוק כפגיעה בשוויון ובכבוד. כאן בית המשפט הלא-נבחר דווקא לא נגע בחקיקת הכנסת — והשאיר מנגנון הדרה לפעול.

החוק: Law for the Prevention of Infiltration (Offenses and Jurisdiction), Amendment No. 4 and Temporary Order, 5774-2013 — establishing the Holot “open” detention facility and one-year closed detention at Saharonim

מקור: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 2504/11 (consolidated with HCJ 2311/11, Sabah v. Knesset) — Sabah v. Knesset (Admissions Committees Law) (2014)

נדחה (אך עם הלכה)

חוק ועדות הקבלה (תיקון מס’ 8 לפקודת האגודות השיתופיות, 2011), המתיר ליישובים קטנים בנגב ובגליל לסנן מועמדים לפי “התאמה חברתית-תרבותית”. בדיון מורחב, ברוב של 5 מול 4, בית המשפט דחה את העתירות מטעמי “היעדר בשלות” — והותיר את החוק שלם וחי. ארבעה שופטים ביקשו לפסול אותו כפגיעה בשוויון ובכבוד; הרוב בחר לא להכריע. כאן הערכאה הלא-נבחרת לא צמצמה את חקיקת הכנסת — היא נמנעה מלגעת בה, והשאירה מנגנון הדרה על כנו.

החוק: Admissions Committees Law — Cooperative Societies Ordinance (Amendment No. 8), 2011, allowing small communities on state land in the Negev and Galilee to vet applicants via admissions committees

מקור: Adalah

בג”ץ 1877/14 (HCJ 1877/14) — Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (2017 conscription ruling) (2017)

בוטל

תיקונים 19 ו-21 לחוק שירות ביטחון — המסגרת שנועדה להסדיר את דחיית גיוסם של בחורי הישיבות החרדים — נפסלו (8 מול 1, בנשיאות נאור) כפגיעה בלתי-מידתית בשוויון ובכבוד האדם. אחד-עשר שופטים לא נבחרו ביטלו חקיקה של הכנסת הנבחרת; תוקף הביטול הושהה בשנה כדי לאפשר חקיקה חלופית.

החוק: Amendments No. 19 (2014) and No. 21 (Nov. 2015) to the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 — the post-Tal-Law framework for deferment/exemption of Haredi yeshiva students

מקור: Afiklaw

בג”ץ 10042/16 — בג”ץ 10042/16 צחי קווטינסקי נ’ כנסת ישראל (Kvatinsky v. Knesset) (2017)

בוטל

מס ריבוי הדירות — “מס הדירה השלישית”, גולת הכותרת של מדיניות הדיור של האוצר — בוטל בידי חמישה שופטים לא-נבחרים (מזוז בדעת מיעוט) לא משום שהמס פסול, אלא בשל פגם בהליך החקיקה: לראשונה אי-פעם, פרק שלם בחוק ההתייעלות הכלכלית הוכרז בטל כי הנוסח הונח בפני חברי הכנסת שעות לפני ההצבעה. בטלות יחסית, והעניין הוחזר לוועדת הכספים לחקיקה מחדש; הכנסת לא חוקקה שוב, והמס פקע.

החוק: פרק י”ב לחוק ההתייעלות הכלכלית (תיקוני חקיקה ליישום המדיניות הכלכלית לשנות התקציב 2017 ו-2018), התשע”ז-2016 — מס ריבוי דירות (Chapter 12 of the Economic Efficiency Law (Legislative Amendments for Budget Years 2017-2018) 5777-2016: the Multiple-Apartment / ‘third home’ tax)

מקור: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

בג”ץ 2293/17 / HCJ 2293/17 — Deposit Law (20% wage deduction) (2020)

בוטל חלקית

הכנסת חוקקה את “חוק הפיקדון” (סעיף 4 לחוק למניעת הסתננות, תיקון לחוק עובדים זרים), שניכה 20% משכר מבקשי המקלט והקפיא אותו עד יציאתם מהארץ — מנוף לחץ כלכלי שנועד לדחוק אותם ל”עזיבה מרצון”. בית המשפט, ברוב של 8 מול 1, פסל את ניכוי שכר העובד כפגיעה לא-מידתית בזכות הקניין, הותיר על כנו רק את חלק המעסיק (16%) והורה להשיב לעובדים את הפיקדונות — ושופטים שלא נבחרו ביטלו את הלב הכופה של חוק נבחרי הציבור.

החוק: Section 4 of the Prevention of Infiltration and Ensuring Departure of Infiltrators from Israel (Legislative Amendments and Temporary Provisions) Law, 5775-2014 (the ‘Deposit Law’), which amended the Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, requiring 20% of asylum seekers’ wages be withheld in a fund payable only on departure

מקור: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 5469/20 (consolidated with HCJ 6312/20, 6786/20, 6859/20, 6866/20, 6878/20) — My Home Is Israel v. Government of Israel (2021-04-04)

בוטל חלקית · הרכב: Nine justices: President E. Hayut, Deputy President H. Melcer, and Justices N. Hendel, U. Vogelman, Y. Amit, N. Sohlberg, D. Barak-Erez, M. Mazuz, A. Baron

תשעה שופטים, בראשות הנשיאה חיות, פסלו את תקנה 24(1) לתקנות הקורונה (חוק סמכויות מיוחדות להתמודדות עם נגיף הקורונה (הוראת שעה), התש”ף-2020), שאסרה הפגנה במרחק העולה על 1,000 מטר ממקום המגורים, כפגיעה בלתי-מידתית בחופש הביטוי ובזכות למחות — בעוד החוק עצמו ורוב הוראותיו אושרו. משהתקנה כבר פקעה (תקפה רק 1–13.10.2020), בית המשפט הבלתי-נבחר הכריז על בטלותה למפרע והורה לבטל ולהשיב את הקנסות שהוטלו על כ-18,000 אזרחים — אלא אם המדינה תחזור בה תוך 90 יום — וכך ריקן מתוכן את אכיפת מגבלת ההפגנות שקבעה הרשות המבצעת.

החוק: Regulation 24(1) of the Special Powers for Coping with the Novel Coronavirus (Temporary Provision) regulations — restricting participation in protests to within 1,000 meters of one’s residence — enacted under the Coronavirus Law (חוק סמכויות מיוחדות להתמודדות עם נגיף הקורונה החדש (הוראת שעה), 5780-2020)

מקור: Adalah

HCJ 6942/19 (בג”ץ 6942/19) — foreign-worker deposit-forfeiture case (2023-07-12)

בוטל · הרכב: Expanded 7-justice panel. Majority (6): President Esther Hayut (authoring), Deputy President Uzi Vogelman, Justices Yitzhak Amit, Daphne Barak-Erez, Anat Baron, Ofer Grosskopf. Dissent (1): Justice Noam Solberg.

החלטות 717, 727 ו-737 של מועצת מקרקעי ישראל העניקו לקיבוצים ולמושבים נתח עצום מערך הקרקע הציבורית בעת הסבת קרקע חקלאית לבנייה ולתעשייה — הקצאה ללא מכרז ובדמי-חכירה מוזלים. שבעה שופטים, שאיש מהם לא נבחר, הורו כי “כל ההחלטות הנידונות בטלות ומבוטלות”, ביטלו במחי יד את מנגנון רווחי-ההון של מגזר ההתיישבות בנימוק של חוסר-סבירות קיצוני ופגיעה בשוויון ובנאמנות הציבורית — וכפו על המועצה לתכנן מסגרת חדשה תחת אמות-מידה שקבע בית המשפט.

החוק: Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, sec. 1A(7)(2) and sec. 1A(7)(3A), and the Foreign Workers (Deposit for Foreign Workers) Regulations, 5776-2016 — the mechanism deducting/forfeiting an escalating share of the worker’s mandatory deposit (severance + employer pension component) if the worker overstayed, with total forfeiture after six months’ delay.

מקור: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

HCJ 6198/23 (lead petition, with joined petitions) — HCJ 6198/23 (and joined petitions) (2024-06-25)

בוטל · הרכב: Extended bench of 9 justices, unanimous; opinion authored by Acting Supreme Court President Justice Uzi Vogelman. (Full roster of all nine names not surfaced in the English-language authoritative sources reviewed.)

משפקעה הסדרת הדחייה לבחורי ישיבה ולא נותר כל פטור בחוק שירות הביטחון, התשמ”ו-1986, פסק בית המשפט פה אחד (תשעה שופטים, מפי המשנה לנשיאה פוגלמן) כי החלטת הממשלה להורות לצה”ל שלא לגייס חרדים זכאי גיוס נעדרת בסיס חוקי: בהיעדר פטור חל חוק חובת הגיוס האוניברסלי, ואין סמכות לאכיפה בררנית. בכך הפך בית המשפט בלתי-נבחר את שתיקת הקואליציה והכנסת לצו שיפוטי פוזיטיבי המחייב לפעול לגיוס ואוסר על העברת כספי מדינה לישיבות עבור תלמידים זכאי גיוס — תוצאה הנוגדת את כוונת הרשות הנבחרת.

החוק: Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 — following expiry (June 30, 2023) of the statutory deferment arrangement and lapse of interim Government Decision 682

מקור: Library of Congress

HCJ 7922/19 (court file 2019-0-7922-60-2) — HCJ 7922/19 Tzedek Financi & Ravach Naki v. State (Chief of Staff Pension “Increases”) (2025-09-15)

בוטל · הרכב: President Isaac Amit (opinion author), Justice Noam Sohlberg/—; confirmed members: President Isaac Amit, Justice David Mintz, Justice Ofer Grosskopf

בית המשפט קבע ש”הגדלות הרמטכ”ל” — תוספות פנסיה של כ-1.8 מיליארד ש”ח בשנה ששולמו לכ-98% מאנשי הקבע — חורגות מסמכות הרמטכ”ל לפי חוק שירות הקבע בצה”ל (גמלאות), שכן הטבה גורפת וכמעט-אוניברסלית היא “הסדר ראשוני” שחייב להיקבע בחקיקת הכנסת ולא בשיקול דעת מינהלי; ביטל את ההסדר (בהשעיה עד סוף 2025, תוך הגנה על גמלאים קיימים) ודחק את הסוגיה אל הכנסת. שופטים בלתי-נבחרים פסלו הטבת-ענק רבת-שנים והכפיפו את מימושה לרצונו הטוב של המחוקק.

החוק: Permanent Service in the IDF (Pensions) Law [Consolidated Version], 5745-1985 — esp. the Chief of Staff’s discretionary pension-‘increase’ power (sec. 18(b))

מקור: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB
patterns.body_html_en:
Reading Down: when the Court empties a law of force — not just when it strikes one

A law the Court empties of content is a law cancelled in practice. After the elected Knesset’s huge effort to legislate, the statute stays on the books — but its power is quietly transferred to unelected hands, against the intent for which it was passed. The damage to the public’s control over its own laws is the same — sometimes worse, because it is done silently. This is a public library of real, verified cases — each with a docket, date, panel, and authoritative source.

48 verified cases (1964–2025) · 25 read down / gutted (not formally struck) · 23 struck or partly struck. Full data: data/library/quiet-veto-cases.json.

What to call it. The plainest, most established English term for this is reading down (Hebrew: פרשנות מצמצמת) — construing a statute so narrowly that almost none of its force survives, without ever striking it down. We lead with that term on purpose: the Court thrives on tangled language, so we answer in the simplest accurate words the field already uses. Related terms, kept secondary: objective purposive interpretation — Aharon Barak’s method of reading a law to mean what the judge thinks it ought to mean; it is the engine of the practice and a larger story we treat on its own. And the Quiet Veto, our shorthand, which borrows a word from Aviad Bakshi — who calls the Court’s power to kill a law a “veto” (JNS, 2020): this is that veto’s silent face. Tellingly, no settled English name exists for the practice — and that is part of the story.

Read down — emptied of force without being struck (reading down · פרשנות מצמצמת)

HCJ 195/64 — HCJ 195/64 Southern Co. (Marbek Slaughterhouse) v. Chief Rabbinical Council (מרבק נ’ המועצה הרבנית הראשית) (1964-09-27)

Read down / gutted · panel: Olshan P., Agranat D.P., Landau J., Witkon J., Manny J.

The Chief Rabbinical Council’s statutory kashrut-certification authority (pre-1983) was left textually intact — but the unelected Court read it down, holding that licence conditions unrelated to the meat’s actual kosher status were ultra vires. The text survived; its real-world reach was hollowed out, stripping the religious authority of power to enforce broader observance on the non-observant and confining it to the food itself.

Statute: Statutory authority of the Chief Rabbinical Council to grant kashrut certification (pre-1983 kashrut authority)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 465/89 (HCJ 465/89) — Raskin v. Religious Council of Jerusalem (1990)

Read down / gutted

The Knesset’s Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983 was about food kashrut alone. Without voiding a single word, the Court read it down to that narrow purpose, holding that the power to grant a kashrut certificate is not a lever for imposing rules of Jewish law unrelated to the food — stripping the Rabbinate of the licensing leverage it had used to condition certification on barring “immodest” belly-dance performances. An unelected bench hollowed the statute’s real-world reach below the Rabbinate’s design, on its own interpretive say-so.

Statute: חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ”ג-1983 — Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983

Source: Versa · Cardozo

דנ”פ 2316/95 — Ganimat v. State of Israel (Further Hearing) (1995)

Read down / gutted

Criminal Procedure Law (sec. 21a): the Knesset let courts jail defendants pre-trial on a fear-to-public-safety ground, which prosecutors turned into near-automatic detention by branding whole crime categories a “national scourge.” Without voiding a single word, the Court read the provision down “in the spirit of” Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty — holding a national scourge alone is no detention ground; the state must prove the individual defendant’s danger. Seven unelected justices hollowed out the legislature’s intent.

Statute: Criminal Procedure Law [Consolidated Version], 5742-1982 — detention-until-end-of-proceedings ground, section 21a(a)(1) (סעיף 21א(א)(1)). (Reported PD 49(4) 589.)

Source: Wikipedia

בג”ץ 4562/92 — Zandberg v. Broadcasting Authority (1996)

Read down / gutted

The Broadcasting Authority (Validation of Radio and TV Fees) Law, 5752-1992 — which the Knesset passed to retroactively validate fee collection an earlier ruling had voided. Barak did not strike the law; he read it down, refusing to apply its retroactive reach to arrears fines on property-rights and no-penalty-in-arrears grounds. This is where the doctrine was minted — “better to narrow a statute by interpretation than to declare part of it void” — the engine by which an unelected court hollows out the elected legislature’s law without voiding a single word of text.

Statute: Broadcasting Authority Law, 5725-1965; Price Stability Law (Temporary Provision), 5745-1985; and the Broadcasting Authority (Validation of Radio and Television Fees) Law, 5752-1992. (Reported PD 50(2) 793.)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 3267/97 (HCJ 3267/97, joined with HCJ 715/98) — Rubinstein v. Minister of Defense (1998)

Read down / gutted

The Defense Service Law (5746-1986): without voiding a single word, the Court read the discretion the statute gave the Defense Minister down to nothing, holding that the sweeping yeshiva-student deferment (“Torato Omanuto” — some 8% of the draft cohort) was a “primary arrangement” only the Knesset may set. Unelected justices thus stripped a decades-old practice of its legal basis, suspending the ruling 12 months to force the elected legislature to act.

Statute: The blanket yeshiva-student (‘Torato Omanuto’) military-service deferment arrangement as administered by the Minister of Defense under the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 (i.e., the deferment granted by executive/administrative discretion rather than by primary legislation)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 5100/94 (consolidated with HCJ 4054/95, 6536/95, 5188/96, 7563/97, 7628/97, 1043/99) — Public Committee Against Torture in Israel v. Government of Israel (1999)

Read down / gutted

The GSS leaned on the Criminal Procedure (Testimony) Ordinance and the Penal Law’s necessity defense as legal cover for “moderate physical pressure” in interrogations. The Court voided no statute — it read them down to nothing: the power to interrogate authorizes no physical means, and necessity is only an after-the-fact defense for an individual, never advance authorization. Unelected justices thus hollowed out the legislative framework the regime relied on, leaving it powerless until the Knesset legislates expressly.

Statute: General Security Service (GSS/Shin Bet) interrogation authority — claimed under the general investigatory power (Criminal Procedure [Testimony] Ordinance, s.2(1)) combined with the necessity defense, Penal Law 5737-1977 s.34(11)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

CA (ע”א) 6024/97 — CA 6024/97 Shavit v. Rishon LeZion Jewish Burial Society (1999-07-06)

Read down / gutted · panel: President A. Barak, Justice M. Cheshin, Justice I. Englard (majority: Cheshin and Barak)

The Right to Alternative Civil Burial Law, 5756-1996 was enacted to widen burial choice — yet the Court refused to read it as overriding the Jewish burial societies whose rabbi barred a family from engraving Gregorian birth/death dates (in Latin numerals) alongside the Hebrew dates on a tombstone. Voiding neither statute nor bylaw, the majority (Cheshin, Barak) instead declared the societies dual public/private bodies bound by public-law norms, read the rule down, and recast the prohibition as an unconstitutional affront to the relatives’ dignity — an unelected court hollowing a religious burial authority’s rule-making into a shell while leaving its text formally intact.

Statute: Burial-society bylaws under religious burial authority; engaging Right to Alternative Civil Burial Law, 5756-1996; Standard Contracts Law; Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty

Source: Versa · Cardozo

CrimFH 7048/97 — Anonymous (Plonim, Lebanese detainees) v. Minister of Defense (2000)

Read down / gutted

The Knesset’s Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law, 5739-1979, section 2, let the executive order administrative detention on state-security grounds. The Court voided no text — in a Further Hearing (Barak, 6-3) it read the section down to permit detention only where the detainee himself poses a danger, hollowing out the holding of Lebanese nationals as “bargaining chips” that the State had built on that same broad wording. Three unelected justices erased an entire executive policy without touching a word the legislature wrote.

Statute: Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law, 5739-1979, section 2

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 953/01 (consolidated with HCJ 1355/01, HCJ 7406/01, HCJ 2283/02) — HCJ 953/01 Solodkin v. Beit Shemesh Municipality (2004-06-14)

Read down / gutted · panel: Nine justices: Aharon Barak (President, author), Theodor Or, Eliahu Mazza, Mishael Cheshin, Jacob Turkel, Dorit Beinisch, Ayala Procaccia, Edmond E. Levy, Miriam Naor

The Local Authorities (Special Authorization) Law, 1956 empowered municipalities to ban pork sales — a grant that on its face permitted blanket prohibitions. Nine justices, led by Barak, struck nothing: they read the authorization down, construing it to require proportionate neighborhood-by-neighborhood balancing, suspended the Beit Shemesh, Carmiel and Tiberias by-laws and remanded them for redrafting — shrinking the power’s real reach far below what the elected legislature had conferred, without voiding a word of the text.

Statute: Local Authorities (Special Authorization) Law, 5717-1956 — the statute empowering municipalities to enact by-laws restricting/prohibiting the sale of pork; and the municipal by-laws of Beit Shemesh, Carmiel and Tiberias issued under it.

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 2597/99; HCJ 2859/99 — HCJ 2597/99 & 2859/99 Rodriguez-Tushbeim v. Minister of Interior (2005-05-31)

Read down / gutted · panel: 11-justice panel: President Barak, Vice-President Emeritus Mazza, Vice-President Cheshin, Justice Emeritus Türkel, and Justices Beinisch, Rivlin, Procaccia, Levy, Grunis, Naor, Hayut. Vote 7-4 (majority: Barak, Mazza, Cheshin, Beinisch, Rivlin, Naor, Hayut; dissent: Türkel, Procaccia, Levy, Grunis).

Section 4B of the Law of Return, 5710-1950, leaves “converted” undefined, and the Interior Ministry read it to require converts to join and belong to the converting community before recognition as Jews. The Supreme Court (11-justice panel, 7-4, 2005) rejected the executive’s narrowing gloss and held that conversion in a recognized Jewish community abroad followed by immigration suffices — an unelected court settling a charged value question and stretching the statute to cover non-Orthodox conversions past the line the elected administration sought to draw.

Statute: Law of Return, 5710-1950, sec. 4B (חוק השבות)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

CrimA 6659/06 (consolidated with CrimA 1757/07, 8228/07, and 3261/08) — A v. State of Israel (CrimA 6659/06) (2008)

Read down / gutted

The Knesset’s Internment of Unlawful Combatants Law, 5762-2002 was built to permit indefinite detention on group-membership grounds, backed by a section 7 presumption that release endangers the state. The Court left the statute formally standing but hollowed it out — reading it to require proof of the detainee’s own individual dangerousness on clear and convincing administrative evidence, rejecting bare membership, and demoting the presumption to a secondary step. An unelected court quietly converted the legislature’s categorical group-detention regime into an individualized-threat one, never voiding a word.

Statute: Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law, 5762-2002 (“Internment of Unlawful Combatants Law”)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 6824/07 — Mana’e v. Tax Authority (מנאע נ’ רשות המסים) (2010-12-20)

Read down / gutted · panel: President Dorit Beinisch, Justice Hanan Melzer, Justice Uzi Fogelman (opinion authored by Fogelman)

The Tax Authority routinely collected tax debts from citizens stopped at police checkpoints — and in malls and other public places — invoking the general collection powers of the Taxes (Collection) Ordinance, 1929. Without voiding a single word, the Court read those powers narrowly and held they contain no explicit authorization to collect a debt at a security roadblock; so unelected justices hollowed out an enforcement tool the legislature handed the executive, leaving the statutory text intact.

Statute: Tax Authority practice of collecting tax debts at police security roadblocks, asserted under general collection/seizure powers in the Tax (Collection) Ordinance and related tax statutes

Source: Wikipedia

HCJ 3809/08 (joined with HCJ 9995/08) — Association for Civil Rights in Israel v. Israel Police (‘Big Brother’ / Communications Data Law) (2012-05-28)

Read down / gutted · panel: Expanded 7-justice panel: President D. Beinisch (author), A. Grunis, E. Rivlin, M. Naor, E. Arbel, E. Hayut, H. Melcer

The Criminal Procedure (Enforcement Powers — Communications Data) Law, 5768-2007 — the “Big Brother” law the Knesset passed to grant police sweeping access to communications metadata. An expanded 7-justice panel under President Beinisch refused to strike it, yet read it so narrowly it left a shell: access was confined to a “focused and concrete purpose” rather than general crime detection, and warrantless access to extreme life-saving cases — an unelected court hollowing out the broad surveillance power the elected legislature intended, without voiding a single word of the text.

Statute: Criminal Procedure (Enforcement Powers — Communications Data) Law, 5768-2007 (חוק סדר הדין הפלילי (סמכויות אכיפה – נתוני תקשורת); ‘חוק האח הגדול’)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 8091/14 — HaMoked: Center for the Defense of the Individual v. Minister of Defense (2014-12-31)

Read down / gutted · panel: Justice Elyakim Rubinstein (authoring), Justice Esther Hayut, Justice Noam Sohlberg

Regulation 119 of the Defence (Emergency) Regulations, 1945 — punitive house demolitions — is shielded by the preservation-of-laws clause, so the Court could not strike it. Instead, unelected justices held the power MUST be construed under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty and exercised proportionately, imposing subtests (severity of offense, harm to innocent family members, strength of evidence, whether sealing or partial demolition would suffice) — converting a sweeping military demolition power into a constrained, individualized one barred from use as collective punishment, all without touching a word of the text.

Statute: Regulation 119 of the Defence (Emergency) Regulations, 1945 (תקנה 119 לתקנות ההגנה (שעת חירום)) — punitive house demolitions

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 5239/11 (consolidated with 5392/11, 5549/11, 2072/12) — בג”ץ 5239/11 אורי אבנרי ואח’ נ’ הכנסת (Avneri v. Knesset (2015)

Read down / gutted

The Boycott Law (5771-2011) was built to deter: anyone publicly calling to boycott Israel is liable, and section 2(c) allowed compensation “without proof of damage.” The elected Knesset legislated — and an unelected court struck 2(c) outright, then read the surviving tort down to demand proof of actual damage plus causation, hollowing out its deterrent bite and leaving the statute standing in name while neutralized in fact.

Statute: Prevention of Harm to the State of Israel by means of Boycott Law, 5771-2011 (החוק למניעת פגיעה במדינת ישראל באמצעות חרם, תשע”א-2011) — the civil-tort provision, section 2.

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

עע”ם 5875/10 (AAA 5875/10) — Conservative (Masorti) Movement & Movement for Progressive Judaism v. Be’er Sheva Religious Council (2016)

Read down / gutted

The Jewish Religious Services Law gave religious councils control over public mikvaot — which they used to bar Reform and Conservative conversion immersions. Voiding nothing in the text, the unelected Court read the statute down: a public mikveh opened for conversion may not discriminate by denominational stream, hollowing out the councils’ use of their statutory authority to entrench the Orthodox monopoly — binding not just Be’er Sheva but every council.

Statute: חוק שירותי הדת היהודיים [נוסח משולב], התשל”א-1971 — Jewish Religious Services (Consolidated Version) Law, 1971 (with the 2000 Prohibition of Discrimination Law and Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 7625/06 (consolidated with בג”ץ 1594/11, בג”ץ 1595/11) — Rogachova v. Ministry of the Interior (2016)

Read down / gutted

Law of Return, 1950: without voiding a word, the justices read section 4B’s term “conversion” to cover private Orthodox-community conversions performed in Israel (for those lawfully resident), overriding the Interior Ministry’s restrictive gloss. An unelected court reset what the statute’s term means in practice — even as the lead petitioner whose name the case bears lost her own claim.

Statute: חוק השבות, התש”י-1950 — Law of Return, 1950 (sec. 4B definition of ‘Jew’ / conversion)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 8101/15 / HCJ 8101/15 — בג”ץ 8101/15 צגטה נ’ שר הפנים / HCJ 8101/15 Tsegeta (Tzegete) v. Minister of Interior (2017)

Read down / gutted

The detention and removal powers under the Entry into Israel Law and Prevention of Infiltration Law were not struck — the Court read them down until they were hollow: because the third-country agreements (Rwanda/Uganda) permit only consensual transfer, the state may neither detain an Eritrean or Sudanese asylum seeker for refusing to leave nor deport him by force. Unelected judges thus froze the forced-departure program the executive had built, without voiding a single word.

Statute: Entry into Israel Law / Prevention of Infiltration Law detention powers, as applied to the government’s ‘voluntary departure’ arrangement deporting Eritrean and Sudanese asylum seekers to unnamed third countries (Rwanda/Uganda)

Source: Hotline

דנג”ץ 5026/16 (Further Hearing on בג”ץ 6494/14) — שי גיני ואח’ נ’ הרבנות הראשית לישראל (Further Hearing (2017)

Read down / gutted

Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983 (sec. 3(a)): without voiding a single word, the justices “read down” the statute until the rule the Knesset enacted was left a near-shell. The Rabbinate’s monopoly over the word “kosher” survived — but the Court carved a “third gate”: an uncertified business may now state truthful facts about who supervises it (even a private body) and admit it holds no Rabbinate certificate, so long as it never says “kosher.” Exactly the private-kashrut path the law meant to foreclose.

Statute: חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ”ג-1983 — Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983, sec. 3(a)

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

HCJ 6494/14 (2016-06-06); Further Hearing דנג”ץ 5026/16 (2017-09-12) — HCJ 6494/14 & HCJ-FH (דנג”ץ) 5026/16 Gini v. Chief Rabbinate (גיני נ’ הרבנות הראשית) (2017-09-12)

Read down / gutted · panel: Expanded 7-justice panel (Further Hearing / דיון נוסף): President M. Naor, Deputy President A. Rubinstein, Deputy President S. Jubran, Justice E. Hayut, Justice H. Melcer, Justice N. Solberg, Justice A. Shaham

The Knesset’s Prohibition of Kashrut Fraud Law, 1983 (sec. 3(a)) barred any kashrut representation without a Rabbinate certificate, and the Rabbinate enforced it as a sweeping monopoly. An expanded seven-justice panel in a Further Hearing voided not a single word — it read the section down so that an uncertified eatery may truthfully state which standards it keeps and how it is supervised, and even disclose that it holds no Rabbinate certificate, so long as it does not call itself “kosher”; thus seven unelected justices hollowed out the enforcement reach and monopoly the elected legislature intended, while leaving the text untouched.

Statute: Prohibition of Kashrut Fraud Law, 5743-1983, sec. 3(a) (חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות)

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

HCJ 2109/20 (consolidated 2135/20, 2141/20) — Ben Meir v. Prime Minister (בן מאיר נ’ ראש הממשלה) (2020-04-26)

Read down / gutted · panel: President E. Hayut, Deputy President H. Melcer, Justice N. Sohlberg

The Government authorized the Shin Bet to track COVID patients’ and contacts’ cell-phone location data under Sec. 7(b)(6) of the GSS Law and emergency regulations issued under Basic Law: The Government — and the unelected Court, without formally voiding the tool, held it could no longer rest on cabinet/administrative authorization, set a short deadline, and forced the executive to dismantle its own emergency mechanism and move the program into primary Knesset legislation, draining the existing regulatory arrangement of effect without striking its text.

Statute: Government’s emergency authorization (per Sec. 7(b)(6) of the General Security Service Law, 5762-2002) plus emergency regulations (תקנות לשעת חירום) under Basic Law: The Government, deploying the Shin Bet (GSS) to track cell-phone location data of COVID-19 patients and their contacts

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 1550/18 (joined with HCJ 2240/18) — Secular Forum Association v. Minister of Health (עמותת הפורום החילוני נ’ שר הבריאות) (2020-04-30)

Struck down · panel: Justices Neal Hendel, Uzi Vogelman, Ofer Grosskopf (Hendel partially dissented on the statutory-authority ground)

Without voiding a single line of legislation, the Court read the Public Health Ordinance (1940) and the Patient’s Rights Law into a shell: Section 33(h) empowers only the Health Ministry’s Director-General — not individual hospitals — to regulate food, and Section 9 of the Patient’s Rights Law grants no authority to ban chametz or search visitors’ bags. Three unelected justices turned a kashrut-enforcing administrative directive into a dead letter, also striking it as an infringement of autonomy, dignity and freedom from religion — gutting by interpretation a policy the system meant to impose.

Statute: Hospital management directives/orders barring visitors from bringing chametz (leavened food) into hospitals during Passover, purportedly grounded in the Public Health Ordinance (פקודת בריאות העם) and the Patient’s Rights Law (חוק זכויות החולה)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 5555/18 (HCJ 5555/18) — בג”ץ 5555/18 Hasson v. Knesset (Nation-State Basic Law) (2021)

Read down / gutted

Basic Law: Israel – The Nation State of the Jewish People (2018) elevated “development of Jewish settlement” to a constitutional value and omitted equality entirely. The Court (Hayut, 10-1) struck nothing — it instead read Section 7 “in harmony” with Human Dignity and Liberty, holding it authorizes no discrimination, so eleven unelected justices hollowed out what the elected Knesset sought to entrench, leaving the clause a power-stripped declaration.

Statute: Basic Law: Israel – The Nation State of the Jewish People (2018), esp. Section 7 (development of Jewish settlement) and Section 1(c) (exclusive Jewish national self-determination)

Source: Library of Congress

בג”ץ 11013/05 (HCJ 11013/05, consolidated petitions) — Reform/Conservative Conversions in Israel (consolidated petitions) v. Minister of the Interior (2021)

Read down / gutted

The Law of Return was left textually intact — but the Court (8-1, CJ Hayut) hollowed out the monopoly the religious establishment sought to preserve: after some 15 years of Knesset inaction, unelected justices held the state could not recognize a Reform or Conservative conversion performed abroad yet reject an identical one performed in Israel, recognizing non-Orthodox (and private non-state Orthodox) conversions done in Israel for the Law of Return — against the intended Orthodox exclusivity.

Statute: חוק השבות, התש”י-1950 — Law of Return, 1950 (recognition of conversion for citizenship)

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

בג”ץ 2412/23 (HCJ 2412/23) — Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (Incapacitation Law) (2024)

Read down / gutted

Amendment No. 12 to Basic Law: The Government (the “Incapacitation Law”) narrowed the grounds for declaring a sitting PM incapacitated to ill-health alone, tailored to shield Netanyahu. The Court (6-5) did not strike it but held it a “personal” misuse of constituent power, “curing” it by interpretation so it applies only from the next Knesset — gutting its entire real-world purpose while leaving the text intact. An unelected bench reduces the legislature’s enactment to an empty shell.

Statute: Amendment No. 12 to Basic Law: The Government (the “Incapacitation Law”), enacted March 2023 — limited the grounds for declaring a sitting PM incapacitated to physical/mental ill-health only, abolishing court-recognized “legal incapacitation” (e.g. via criminal proceedings / conflict-of-interest violations).

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

Struck down / partly struck

HCJ 910/86 — Ressler v. Minister of Defence (רסלר נ’ שר הביטחון) (1988-06-12)

Warning of voidness · panel: Shamgar P., Ben-Porat D.P., Barak J. (three-justice panel)

Petition denied: the Court held that the yeshiva-student service-deferment arrangement (“Torato Umanuto”), exercised under the Defense Minister’s administrative discretion per the Defense Service Law, lay within his authority — striking nothing down. Yet three unelected justices reserved a judicial ceiling over an arrangement the elected branches left open-ended: Barak warned that “quantity becomes quality,” and once the deferred grow to a significant portion of conscription candidates the arrangement turns unreasonable and must be struck down — converting an elected-branch prerogative into a power the bench may void on a clock of its own setting.

Statute: Yeshiva-student military-service deferment arrangement issued under the Defense Service Law (administrative deferment of full-time yeshiva students, ‘Torato Umanuto’)

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 1715/97 — בג”ץ 1715/97 לשכת מנהלי ההשקעות בישראל נ’ שר האוצר (Investment Managers Bureau in Israel v. Minister of Finance) (1997)

Partly struck

The first time since the “constitutional revolution” that unelected judges struck a Knesset statute: sec. 48 of the 1995 Investment Advice and Portfolio Management Law — which forced veteran advisers and managers to sit licensing exams to keep practicing — was partially voided as a disproportionate violation of freedom of occupation, compelling the legislature to soften its transitional regime and blunting the uniform licensing barrier it had enacted. (Other provisions, like the bar on managing family portfolios, were upheld.)

Statute: חוק הסדרת העיסוק בייעוץ השקעות ובניהול תיקי השקעות, התשנ”ה-1995, סעיף 48 (Regulation of Investment Advice and Portfolio Management Law 5755-1995, sec. 48 — transitional licensing provisions)

HCJ 6055/95 (בג”ץ 6055/95), consolidated with HCJ 7083/95 — Tzemach v. Minister of Defense (1999)

Struck down

Section 237A(a) of the Military Adjudication Law, 1955 — which let the army detain a soldier up to 96 hours before a judge — was struck down as a disproportionate breach of liberty under the Basic Law, with invalidity suspended 12 months. Eleven unelected justices thus forced the Knesset to halve the detention power it had legislated, from 96 hours to 48.

Statute: Military Justice (Adjudication) Law, 5715-1955 — §237A(a), authorizing detention of a soldier for up to 96 hours before being brought before a military judge.

בג”ץ 6698/95 (HCJ 6698/95) — בג”ץ 6698/95 (HCJ 6698/95) Ka’adan v. Israel Land Administration (Katzir) (2000)

Read down / gutted

No Knesset statute fell here — an administrative practice did: in 2000 the Court under Barak held that channeling state land to the Jewish Agency to build the communal settlement of Katzir on a Jews-only basis violates equality, and ordered the Israel Land Administration to reconsider the Arab petitioners’ application. A foundational ruling whose real-world enforcement stayed slow and contested for years.

Statute: No Knesset statute struck; challenge to State/Israel Land Administration policy of allocating state land to the Jewish Agency for exclusively-Jewish community settlements (Katzir). Constrains the operative reach of land-allocation practice rather than a statute.

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 244/00 (בג”ץ 244/00), consolidated with five further petitions — HCJ 244/00 Mizrahi Democratic Rainbow (“Keshet”) / Siyach Chadash Association for Democratic Discourse v. Minister of National Infrastructure (2002-08-29)

Struck down · panel: Panel of seven justices: President Aharon Barak (presiding); Justices Theodor Or, Eliyahu Matza, Tova Strasberg-Cohen, Dalia Dorner, Dorit Beinisch, and Ayala Procaccia. Lead opinion authored by Justice Theodor Or.

Israel Lands Administration Law / Basic Law: Israel Lands: a panel of seven unelected justices declared “all the discussed decisions null and void,” striking Israel Land Council Decisions 717, 727 and 737 — the windfall mechanism that would have funneled a vast share of the value of rezoned state agricultural land to the settlement sector. The Court branded the scheme extremely unreasonable and a breach of equality and the state’s trusteeship over a public resource, forcing the Land Council and Knesset to redraw the framework from scratch.

Statute: Israel Land Council Decisions (החלטות מועצת מקרקעי ישראל) No. 717, 727 and 737 — secondary land-allocation policy issued under the Israel Lands Administration Law / Basic Law: Israel Lands. Decision 727 gave kibbutzim/moshavim leasing agricultural state land 27–29% of state revenue when the land was rezoned for residential use; Decision 737 let settlements rezone and re-allocate land to members/recommendees without tender at reduced lease fees (22–66% of value); Decision 717 allowed discounted lease rates (16–51% of value) for industrial rezoning.

Source: Wikipedia

HCJ 3239/02 — Marab v. IDF Commander in the West Bank (2003)

Partly struck

Not a Knesset statute — but IDF military orders (Order No. 1500 and its amendments), issued by the area commander under occupation emergency powers. The Court did not abolish the wartime detention authority, yet declared void as disproportionate the long detention periods without judicial review (18/12 days) and the prolonged pre-interrogation delay — suspending the ruling ~6 months — cutting down the orders’ operative reach, while upholding the restrictions on lawyer access.

Statute: NOTE: Not a Knesset statute. The instruments struck down were IDF military orders — the Order regarding Arrests During Times of War (Provisional Order) (West Bank), Order No. 1500 and its amendments 1505/1512/1518 — issued under the area commander’s emergency/belligerent-occupation powers.

HCJ 1437/02 (consolidated with HCJ 3168/02) — HCJ 1437/02 Association for Civil Rights in Israel (ACRI) v. Minister of Public Security (2004 (official reporter IsrSC 58(2) 746; precise day unconfirmed — a search surfaced “10 February 2004” but not grounded in a primary source))

Struck down · panel: Justice Esther Hayut (majority) and Justice Eliezer Rivlin (majority); Justice Miriam Naor (dissent)

Regulation 29(b) of the Prison Service Regulations, 5738-1978 — empowering the prison authority to bar or restrict a prisoner’s meeting with his lawyer — was struck down: the Court declared it void for want of authority under the Prisons Ordinance and as a disproportionate infringement of the right to counsel, yet suspended the voidness for roughly nine months to let the Knesset legislate. Unelected justices erased a secondary arrangement the prison service had wielded and forced the executive and legislature to move the matter into primary law.

HCJ 1661/05 (consolidated petitions) — reported IsrSC 59(2) 481 (2005) — Gaza Coast Regional Council v. The Knesset (2005-06-09)

Partly struck · panel: 11-justice expanded panel headed by President Aharon Barak; petition on the core scheme rejected 10-1 (compensation provisions modified/partly invalidated)

Implementation of the Disengagement Plan Law, 2005: an 11-justice panel under Aharon Barak upheld the evacuation scheme itself (10-1) but partially struck the compensation arrangement as a disproportionate violation of property and dignity — extending the individual grant to those under 21, fixing the residence-duration calculation, and loosening restrictions that confined evacuees to the statutory compensation track alone. Statute: Implementation of the Disengagement Plan Law, 2005 — compensation provisions

בג”ץ 6427/02 (HCJ 6427/02), 61(1) PD 619, decided 11 May 2006 — Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (2006 Tal Law monitoring ruling) (2006)

Dismissed (w/ doctrine)

The Tal Law (Deferment of Military Service for Yeshiva Students, 5762-2002) faced review — and this time the Court chose not to act: a nine-justice panel acknowledged the statute harmed equality and human dignity, yet declined to strike it, granting the State more time to prove it would raise Haredi enlistment. Judicial power that, in its very restraint, left a de facto blanket-exemption regime intact until the 2012 strike-down.

HCJ 4542/02 — Kav LaOved Worker’s Hotline v. Government of Israel (2006-03-30)

Struck down · panel: President A. Barak, Vice-President Emeritus M. Cheshin, Justice E.E. Levy (opinion by Levy)

In Kav LaOved (2006) the Court struck down the government’s “binding arrangement” issued under section 6 of the Entry into Israel Law, 5712-1952 — which chained a migrant worker to one named employer, so that leaving turned the worker into an illegal alien facing arrest and deportation. בג”ץ 8300/02 (lead; consolidated with בג”ץ 7742/03, 6880/05, 6901/05, 8371/05, 463/06, 4659/06, 6832/07 — petitions filed 2002–2007) — בג”ץ 8300/02 גדבאן נסר נ’ ממשלת ישראל (Gadban Nasser et al. v. Government of Israel) (2012)

Partly struck

Income Tax Ordinance — the income-tax-benefit mechanism built on a list of communities the Knesset itself hand-picked (First Schedule Part B, Amendment 146). HCJ 10662/04 (joined with HCJ 3282/05 and HCJ 7804/05) — בג”ץ 10662/04 סלאח חסן נ’ המוסד לביטוח לאומי; official citation PD 65(1) 782. — HCJ 10662/04 Salah Hassan v. National Insurance Institute (2012-02-28)

Struck down · panel: Seven-justice panel led by President Dorit Beinisch, with Justices Miriam Naor, Edna Arbel, Elyakim Rubinstein, Salim Joubran, Esther Hayut, and Uzi Fogelman. (Notably Beinisch’s final judgment before retiring.)

A seven-justice bench led by President Beinisch struck down section 9A(b) of the Income Support (Guaranteed Income) Law, 5741-1980 — the conclusive presumption disqualifying any vehicle owner/user from the income-guarantee benefit — holding that categorically denying a minimum of dignified subsistence disproportionately violates human dignity. Statute: Income Support (Guaranteed Income) Law, 5741-1980 — sec. 9A(b), the conclusive presumption disqualifying any owner/user of a private vehicle from the income-guarantee benefit

AAA 3782/12 (עע”מ 3782/12) — Tel Aviv-Jaffa District Commander v. Israel Internet Association (2013-03-24)

Struck down · panel: Justice Uzi Vogelman (majority opinion); President Asher Grunis (concurring); Justice Noam Sohlberg (dissenting)

Police had arrogated to themselves a power to order ISPs to block gambling sites, stretching the authority to close a “place” under sec. 229 of the Penal Law, 5737-1977; the Supreme Court dismissed the State’s appeal and held — even while accepting that a website might be a “place” and blocking a “closure” — that no express statute authorized conscripting a private third party into enforcement, voiding a power the legislature never granted and sending the police back to the Knesset for primary legislation. One justice (Sohlberg) dissented, finding the authority did exist.
