בג”ץ 7385/13 — Eitan (2014)

Partly struck

Amendment No. 4 to the Prevention of Infiltration Law (2013) built a deterrence machine against asylum seekers: a year of closed detention plus open-ended confinement at “Holot.” The Court (President Naor) struck both pillars — section 30A and the entire Holot chapter — voiding the elected legislature’s second attempt and suspending the annulment 90 days for the Knesset to dismantle it.

Statute: Prevention of Infiltration (Offences and Jurisdiction) Law, 5714-1954, as amended by Amendment No. 4 (5774-2013) — detention/residency-center provisions (notably section 30A and the ‘Holot’ open-facility chapter).

Source: Versa · Cardozo

בג”ץ 8425/13 (consolidated with בג”ץ 7385/13) / HCJ 8425/13 — Eitan (2014)

Partly struck

The Admissions Committees Law (Cooperative Societies Ordinance Amendment No. 8, 2011), letting small Negev and Galilee communities screen applicants for “social-cultural suitability,” was left wholly intact. By 5-4 the justices dismissed the petitions as unripe — neither striking nor narrowing the statute — while four would have voided it as a violation of equality and dignity. Here the unelected court chose NOT to touch the Knesset’s law, leaving a gatekeeping mechanism standing.

Statute: Law for the Prevention of Infiltration (Offenses and Jurisdiction), Amendment No. 4 and Temporary Order, 5774-2013 — establishing the Holot “open” detention facility and one-year closed detention at Saharonim

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 2504/11 (consolidated with HCJ 2311/11, Sabah v. Knesset) — Sabah v. Knesset (Admissions Committees Law) (2014)

Dismissed (w/ doctrine)

The Admissions Committees Law (Amendment No. 8 to the Cooperative Societies Ordinance, 2011) lets small Negev and Galilee communities screen applicants for “social-cultural suitability.” Sitting expanded, the Court 5-4 DISMISSED the petitions as “unripe” — leaving the statute fully intact. Four justices would have struck it as a violation of equality and dignity; the majority chose not to rule. Here the unelected court did not narrow the Knesset’s law — it declined to touch it, leaving a gatekeeping mechanism standing.

Statute: Admissions Committees Law — Cooperative Societies Ordinance (Amendment No. 8), 2011, allowing small communities on state land in the Negev and Galilee to vet applicants via admissions committees

Source: Adalah

בג”ץ 1877/14 (HCJ 1877/14) — Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (2017 conscription ruling) (2017)

Struck down

Amendments 19 and 21 to the Defense Service Law — the framework regulating deferment of Haredi yeshiva students’ conscription — were struck down (8-1, President Naor) as a disproportionate violation of equality and human dignity. Unelected justices voided the elected Knesset’s statute; the declaration of invalidity was suspended one year for replacement legislation.

Statute: Amendments No. 19 (2014) and No. 21 (Nov. 2015) to the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 — the post-Tal-Law framework for deferment/exemption of Haredi yeshiva students

Source: Afiklaw

בג”ץ 10042/16 — בג”ץ 10042/16 צחי קווטינסקי נ’ כנסת ישראל (Kvatinsky v. Knesset) (2017)

Struck down

The multiple-apartment “third home” tax — the centerpiece of the Finance Minister’s housing policy — was nullified by five unelected justices (Mazuz dissenting) not because the tax was unconstitutional, but for a defect in the legislative process: for the first time ever, an entire chapter of the Economic Efficiency Law was voided because MKs got the final text mere hours before the vote. A relative nullification, returned to the Finance Committee to re-enact; the Knesset never did, and the tax lapsed.

Statute: פרק י”ב לחוק ההתייעלות הכלכלית (תיקוני חקיקה ליישום המדיניות הכלכלית לשנות התקציב 2017 ו-2018), התשע”ז-2016 — מס ריבוי דירות (Chapter 12 of the Economic Efficiency Law (Legislative Amendments for Budget Years 2017-2018) 5777-2016: the Multiple-Apartment / ‘third home’ tax)

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

בג”ץ 2293/17 / HCJ 2293/17 — Garsegeber v. The Knesset (2020)

Partly struck

The Knesset enacted the “Deposit Law” (Section 4 of the Prevention of Infiltration Law, amending the Foreign Workers Law), withholding 20% of asylum seekers’ wages until they left the country — economic coercion engineered to push them toward “self-deportation.” By 8-1 the Court partially struck the statute, voiding the employee wage-deduction as a disproportionate infringement of property rights while leaving the 16% employer contribution standing, and ordered the withheld deposits returned — unelected judges gutting the elected legislature’s coercive core.

Statute: Section 4 of the Prevention of Infiltration and Ensuring Departure of Infiltrators from Israel (Legislative Amendments and Temporary Provisions) Law, 5775-2014 (the ‘Deposit Law’), which amended the Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, requiring 20% of asylum seekers’ wages be withheld in a fund payable only on departure

Source: Versa · Cardozo

HCJ 5469/20 (consolidated with HCJ 6312/20, 6786/20, 6859/20, 6866/20, 6878/20) — National Responsibility (2021-04-04)

Partly struck · panel: Nine justices: President E. Hayut, Deputy President H. Melcer, and Justices N. Hendel, U. Vogelman, Y. Amit, N. Sohlberg, D. Barak-Erez, M. Mazuz, A. Baron

A nine-justice bench under President Hayut struck down Regulation 24(1) of the Coronavirus emergency regulations (enacted under the Special Powers for Coping with the Novel Coronavirus (Temporary Provision) Law, 5780-2020), which barred protesting more than 1,000 meters from one’s home, as a disproportionate violation of free expression and the right to protest — while upholding the Law itself and most of its provisions. Since the regulation had already expired (in force only Oct 1-13, 2020), the unelected Court declared it void retroactively and ordered the cancellation and refund of fines levied on some 18,000 citizens unless the state rescinded them within 90 days, hollowing out enforcement of the executive’s protest-limiting rule.

Statute: Regulation 24(1) of the Special Powers for Coping with the Novel Coronavirus (Temporary Provision) regulations — restricting participation in protests to within 1,000 meters of one’s residence — enacted under the Coronavirus Law (חוק סמכויות מיוחדות להתמודדות עם נגיף הקורונה החדש (הוראת שעה), 5780-2020)

Source: Adalah

HCJ 6942/19 (בג”ץ 6942/19) — HCJ 6942/19 Tzebano (Kav LaOved & ACRI) v. Minister of Interior / Knesset (2023-07-12)

Struck down · panel: Expanded 7-justice panel. Majority (6): President Esther Hayut (authoring), Deputy President Uzi Vogelman, Justices Yitzhak Amit, Daphne Barak-Erez, Anat Baron, Ofer Grosskopf. Dissent (1): Justice Noam Solberg.

Israel Land Council Decisions 717, 727 and 737 handed kibbutzim and moshavim a vast share of the state-land value created when farmland was rezoned for housing and industry — tender-free allocation at slashed lease fees. Seven unelected justices declared “all the decisions discussed null and void,” wiping out the settlement sector’s windfall mechanism for extreme unreasonableness and breach of equality and the state’s trusteeship of public land — forcing the Council to rebuild the framework under court-set criteria.

Statute: Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, sec. 1A(7)(2) and sec. 1A(7)(3A), and the Foreign Workers (Deposit for Foreign Workers) Regulations, 5776-2016 — the mechanism deducting/forfeiting an escalating share of the worker’s mandatory deposit (severance + employer pension component) if the worker overstayed, with total forfeiture after six months’ delay.

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

HCJ 6198/23 (lead petition, with joined petitions) — Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. Minister of Defense (2024-06-25)

Struck down · panel: Extended bench of 9 justices, unanimous; opinion authored by Acting Supreme Court President Justice Uzi Vogelman. (Full roster of all nine names not surfaced in the English-language authoritative sources reviewed.)

With the yeshiva-deferment arrangement expired and no exemption left in the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986, a unanimous nine-justice bench (opinion by Acting President Vogelman) held the government’s instruction to the IDF not to draft draft-eligible Haredim legally baseless: absent any exemption the universal conscription duty applies and selective non-enforcement is unlawful. An unelected court thus converted the coalition’s and Knesset’s deliberate inaction into an affirmative judicial command to draft and a bar on transferring state funds to yeshivas for draft-eligible students — a result running directly against the elected branches’ intent.

Statute: Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 — following expiry (June 30, 2023) of the statutory deferment arrangement and lapse of interim Government Decision 682

Source: Library of Congress

HCJ 7922/19 (court file 2019-0-7922-60-2) — HCJ 7922/19 Tzedek Financi & Ravach Naki v. State (Chief of Staff Pension “Increases”) (2025-09-15)

Struck down · panel: President Isaac Amit (opinion author), Justice Noam Sohlberg/—; confirmed members: President Isaac Amit, Justice David Mintz, Justice Ofer Grosskopf

The Court struck down the “Chief of Staff increases” — pension top-ups of roughly NIS 1.8 billion a year paid to some 98% of career personnel — as exceeding the Chief of Staff’s authority under the Permanent Service (Pensions) Law, holding that a blanket, near-universal benefit is a “primary arrangement” the Knesset must legislate, not executive discretion; it voided the scheme (suspended to end-2025, sparing existing retirees) and pushed the matter to the legislature. Unelected justices canceled a decades-old mass entitlement and made its survival contingent on lawmakers’ goodwill.

Statute: Permanent Service in the IDF (Pensions) Law [Consolidated Version], 5745-1985 — esp. the Chief of Staff’s discretionary pension-‘increase’ power (sec. 18(b))

Source: מאגר פסקי הדין הרשמי / Official court DB

structure.title: How Power Was Concentrated: Seven Structural Mechanisms
structure.title_he: כך רוכז הכוח: שבעה מנגנונים מבניים
structure.title_en: How Power Was Concentrated: Seven Structural Mechanisms
structure.summary: Seven procedural mechanisms by which decision-making power moved from elected officials to an unelected layer of judges and legal advisors — each shown first by the documented outcome it produced, then by the mechanism itself.
structure.summary_he: שבעה מנגנונים פרוצדורליים שבאמצעותם עבר כוח ההכרעה מנבחרי הציבור אל שכבה לא-נבחרה של שופטים ויועצים משפטיים — כל אחד מוצג תחילה דרך התוצאה המתועדת שהוא הוליד, ואחר כך דרך המנגנון עצמו.
structure.summary_en: Seven procedural mechanisms by which decision-making power moved from elected officials to an unelected layer of judges and legal advisors — each shown first by the documented outcome it produced, then by the mechanism itself.
structure.body_html:
This chapter is not about a single ruling but about a structure: a set of procedural mechanisms through which decision-making power moved from elected officials to an unelected layer of judges and legal advisors. Each mechanism is presented in two parts — what already happened (the documented outcome it produced), then the mechanism (how it actually works, and its legal basis). The procedural facts are verifiable from the law and from how the system operates; the judgment that this amounts to a concentration of power is the project’s assessment, anchored in the disclosed facts beside it.

1. The apex court as the court of first instance — and last

What already happened. On 18 January 2023 the High Court of Justice voided, 10–1, the appointment of Aryeh Deri as Minister of the Interior and of Health, holding it “unreasonable in the extreme.” Prime Minister Netanyahu said he was “compelled, with a heavy heart” to dismiss him, and did so by cabinet vote days later. The government had no avenue of appeal: the HCJ is both the first and the last judicial word. (The ruling, in this database: HCJ 8948/22 Deri.)

The mechanism. Section 15 of Basic Law: The Judiciary provides that the Supreme Court also sits as the High Court of Justice, hearing petitions against state authorities directly. The same body is thus both the state’s final court of appeal and the first-instance forum where a citizen challenges a minister’s or government’s decision — with no appeal from its decision. In every other democracy with judicial review of executive action, the first-instance ruling can be appealed to a higher court that corrects error and moderates a single bench’s preferences. In Israel the HCJ is the Supreme Court: the government faces a single adjudication, before the most powerful bench in the country, with no further legal recourse. The Court did the same on 1 January 2024, when a full bench of 15 justices reviewed and struck down an amendment to a Basic Law (see §7) — also at first and last instance.

2. Direct access to the apex — bypassing the lower courts

What already happened. In August 2019, after the justice minister fired the ministry’s director-general and moved to install a political associate, a petition was filed directly with the HCJ, and within days the appointment was frozen by interim order. No administrative tribunal or district-court step preceded it, and no appeal was possible — the HCJ was the only forum from the first moment. (Source: Jerusalem Post and Haaretz reporting, August 2019.)

The mechanism. Petitions on the most significant national matters — government decisions, regulations, appointments, legislation — go straight to the Supreme Court. The 2000 Administrative Courts Law moved enumerated categories to district courts, but everything not expressly listed stays with the HCJ via a catch-all clause. In most legal systems a challenger must exhaust remedies, pass a specialist tribunal, a general court, and an appellate tier — and the government governs throughout. In Israel, for the retained categories, there is no exhaustion requirement and no lower-court filter: an appointment announced Monday can be frozen by the HCJ by Friday. The government cannot use procedural time as a political resource — which also amplifies the Attorney General’s power (§3), since the threat of rapid intervention makes the AG’s opinion costly to ignore.

3. The chain of legal advisors — binding opinions, not advice

What already happened. In 2025 the Attorney General announced she would not represent the government in petitions concerning the Prime Minister’s refusal to establish a state commission of inquiry into the events of 7 October, and that the government would have to seek its own private counsel. The government’s own legal arm opposed its position, and the Prime Minister had to find private lawyers to appear in the country’s highest court — against his own Attorney General. (Source: Times of Israel and Ynet reporting, 2025.)

The mechanism. The Attorney General’s binding authority has no explicit statutory basis — it was built from AG Shamgar’s 1968 self-declaration that the role is “of a judicial nature,” crystallized in Justice Barak’s 1993 Deri-Pinhasi ruling that the AG’s opinion “reflects the positive law,” and operationalized through government resolutions. To this is added a monopoly on representation: the AG decides what arguments the State of Israel presents in court. If the AG deems the government’s position “indefensible,” the government’s only options are to abandon it, or to appear in its own name with private counsel — with no legal apparatus, against its own AG. Ministry legal advisors — professionally subordinate to the AG, not to the minister — can refuse to draft an opinion supporting a decision they deem unlawful, and the minister cannot dismiss them. The entire structure — granting an appointed official a veto over the government’s legal strategy — rests on judicial interpretation and government resolutions, not on a statute the Knesset enacted.

4. Panel composition — who decides which justices hear the case

What already happened. HCJ 5658/23, in which an amendment to a Basic Law was struck down, was heard by an expanded panel of 15 justices — and decided 8 to 7. The size of the panel determined the outcome: on a smaller panel the majority might have flipped.

The mechanism. Assignment is computerized by default, but the Court President holds sole discretion over panel size — expanding it “to any odd number” in matters of particular importance. As academic critique notes (Federalist Society Review), choosing seven justices instead of five can decide a charged constitutional case. An empirical study (Givati & Rosenberg, Journal of Empirical Legal Studies, 2020) found that justices a sitting President had earlier selected sit on her panels more often — consistent with strategic composition. Outcome-affecting discretion in the most sensitive cases is thus concentrated in one office. (This is not a claim that the President hand-picks every panel; the default is random, and the effect runs through panel size and the exception.)

5. No standing requirement — anyone, on almost anything

What already happened. In 2006 the HCJ struck down the “Tal Law” (military-service deferment for yeshiva students) on a petition by the Movement for Quality Government — a civic NGO whose members suffer no personal injury from the deferment arrangement. Legislation backed by a governing coalition was voided on the petition of a body with no personal stake. The same arrangement was struck down again and again afterward (2012, 2017, 2024), each time on petitions by organizations with no personal injury. (Source: HCJ 6427/02; VERSA, Cardozo.)

The mechanism. In HCJ 910/86 Ressler v. Minister of Defence (1988), Justice Barak reduced the standing requirement almost to nothing and created a parallel track: even without personal injury, standing is granted where the matter is of “particular public importance” and touches the rule of law. As VERSA notes, the Court “effectively removed [standing] as a threshold.” The result is structural: organized, well-funded organizations can use the HCJ as a continuous policy-review mechanism against any government — and the parliamentary majority provides no shield, because the Court itself decides whether standing is met.

6. Judicial self-selection — and the case the project is named after

What already happened. Justice Minister Yariv Levin sought to appoint to the Supreme Court Aviad Bakshi and Raphael Bitton — two figures identified with the 2023 reform. Lacking the required supermajority (7 of 9) for those appointments, a deadlock set in: the minister declined to convene the committee to appoint other justices, and three Supreme Court seats stood vacant for an extended period, contributing to a severe judge shortage across the court system. (The deadlock was two-sided: both the minister’s refusal to convene and the committee members’ votes against the candidates produced it. Sources: Times of Israel; IDI.) This project’s name — Bakshi & Bitton — is taken from this episode.

The mechanism. The Judicial Selection Committee has nine members, three of them sitting Supreme Court justices. Since 2008 a supermajority of 7 of 9 is required to appoint Supreme Court justices. The asymmetry is structural: blocking a candidate takes only five votes; appointing one takes seven. Over time this draws the Court’s composition toward candidates acceptable to the sitting justices, not necessarily to the elected government of the day — the layer reproducing itself and diminishing elected officials’ weight in its composition. (In 2025 the supermajority structure was changed by contested legislation, now deferred and under constitutional review by the Court itself.)

7. The Court defines the limits of its own jurisdiction — “everything is justiciable”

What already happened. On 1 January 2024 the Court, sitting as a full bench of 15, struck down Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary — the amendment that sought to remove reasonableness review of elected-branch decisions. By 12–3 it held, in principle, that it has authority to review amendments to Basic Laws; by 8–7 it voided this specific amendment. The Knesset’s attempt to narrow the Court’s review power was thus annulled by the Court itself, under a theory of jurisdiction the Court devised for itself, with no avenue of appeal or override. (The ruling, in this database: HCJ 5658/23.)

The mechanism. Justice Barak’s “everything is justiciable” doctrine, together with the reversal of the “political question” doctrine in Ressler (1988), expanded review so that almost any matter — including policy, security, and appointment decisions once treated as “political” — is open to judicial review. The structural feature: there is no external body that can tell the Court it lacks jurisdiction. The Court decides what is justiciable, and its justiciability rulings are not themselves reviewable. It is a one-way ratchet: the Court expanded justiciability, and when the Knesset tried to narrow it by statute, the Court voided the narrowing under its self-declared power to review even Basic Laws.

A note on method

The descriptions of the mechanisms above are procedural facts, verifiable from the legal basis, the case law, and how the system operates; each outcome is given with its source. The judgment that this amounts to a concentration of power and a weakening of elected government is the project’s assessment — an opinion anchored in the disclosed facts beside it, not a factual claim about any individual’s state of mind or motive. We have deliberately avoided the sweeping comparative claim (“nothing like it anywhere”): the structural fact itself carries the argument. The figures named here are named solely in connection with their public, documented acts in their official roles.
structure.body_html_he:
הפרק הזה אינו עוסק בפסיקה בודדת אלא במבנה: בסדרת מנגנונים פרוצדורליים שבאמצעותם הועבר כוח הכרעה מנבחרי הציבור אל שכבה לא-נבחרה של שופטים ויועצים משפטיים. כל מנגנון מוצג כאן בשני חלקים — מה כבר קרה (התוצאה המתועדת שהוא הוליד), ואחריו המנגנון (כיצד הוא פועל בפועל, ומה בסיסו המשפטי). העובדות הפרוצדורליות ניתנות לאימות מן הדין ומאופן פעולת המערכת; הקביעה שמדובר בריכוז כוח היא הערכת הפרויקט, מעוגנת בעובדות הגלויות שלצידה.

1. הערכאה העליונה כערכאה ראשונה — ואחרונה

מה כבר קרה. ב-18 בינואר 2023 ביטל בג”ץ ברוב 10–1 את מינויו של אריה דרעי לשר הפנים והבריאות. בית המשפט קבע כי המינוי “בלתי-סביר בעליל”. ראש הממשלה נתניהו הודיע כי הוא “נאלץ, בלב כבד” לפטר אותו, ועשה זאת בהצבעת ממשלה ימים אחדים לאחר מכן. לא היה לממשלה נתיב ערעור: בג”ץ הוא גם המילה הראשונה וגם המילה האחרונה. (הפסיקה במאגר זה: בג”ץ 8948/22 דרעי.)

המנגנון. סעיף 15 לחוק-יסוד: השפיטה קובע שבית המשפט העליון יושב גם כבית משפט גבוה לצדק ודן ישירות בעתירות נגד רשויות המדינה. משמעות הדבר: אותו גוף עצמו הוא גם ערכאת הערעור הסופית של המדינה וגם הערכאה הראשונה שבפניה אזרח תוקף החלטת שר או ממשלה — ואין ערעור על החלטתו. בכל דמוקרטיה אחרת שבה קיימת ביקורת שיפוטית על פעולה שלטונית, הכרעת הערכאה הראשונה ניתנת לערעור לערכאה גבוהה יותר, המתקנת טעויות וממתנת את תוצאת העדפותיו של מותב יחיד. בישראל, בג”ץ הוא בית המשפט העליון: הממשלה ניצבת מול הכרעה אחת, בפני המותב החזק במדינה, ללא כל מוצא משפטי נוסף. כך עשה בית המשפט גם ב-1 בינואר 2024, כשהרכב מלא של 15 שופטים בחן וביטל תיקון לחוק-יסוד (ראו §7 להלן) — אף הוא בערכאה ראשונה ואחרונה.

2. גישה ישירה לפסגה — דילוג על הערכאות הנמוכות

מה כבר קרה. באוגוסט 2019, לאחר ששר המשפטים דאז פיטר את מנכ”לית משרד המשפטים וביקש למנות במקומה מקורב פוליטי, הוגשה עתירה ישירות לבג”ץ, ובתוך ימים אחדים הוקפא המינוי בצו זמני. לא קדם לכך כל הליך בערכאה מינהלית או מחוזית, ולא היה ערעור — בג”ץ היה הפורום היחיד מן הרגע הראשון. (מקור: דיווחי Jerusalem Post ו-Haaretz, אוגוסט 2019.)

המנגנון. עתירות בג”ץ בעניינים הלאומיים המשמעותיים — החלטות ממשלה, תקנות, מינויים, חקיקה — מגיעות ישירות לבית המשפט העליון. חוק בתי משפט לעניינים מינהליים, תש”ס-2000, העביר קטגוריות מנויות לבתי משפט מחוזיים, אך כל מה שלא נמנה במפורש נותר בבג”ץ מכוח סעיף-סל. במרבית שיטות המשפט, מי שתוקף פעולה שלטונית חייב למצות הליכים, לעבור טריבונל ייעודי, ערכאה כללית ושלב ערעור — והממשלה פועלת בכל אותו זמן. בישראל, לקטגוריות שנותרו, אין דרישת מיצוי ואין סינון מלמטה: מינוי שהוכרז ביום שני יכול להיות מוקפא בידי בג”ץ עד יום שישי. הממשלה אינה יכולה להשתמש בזמן ההליך כמשאב פוליטי — וגם זה מגביר את כוחו של היועץ המשפטי (§3), שכן איום ההתערבות המהירה הופך את חוות-דעתו לכזו שיקר להתעלם ממנה.

3. שרשרת היועצים המשפטיים — חוות-דעת מחייבת, לא מייעצת

מה כבר קרה. ב-2025 הודיעה היועצת המשפטית לממשלה כי לא תייצג את הממשלה בעתירות הנוגעות לסירובו של ראש הממשלה להקים ועדת חקירה ממלכתית לאירועי 7 באוקטובר, וכי על הממשלה לבקש ייצוג פרטי משלה. כלומר: זרועה המשפטית של הממשלה התנגדה לעמדתה, וראש הממשלה נדרש לחפש עורכי-דין פרטיים שיופיעו בערכאה הגבוהה במדינה — מול היועצת המשפטית שלו עצמו. (מקור: דיווחי Times of Israel ו-Ynet, 2025.)

המנגנון. לסמכותו המחייבת של היועץ המשפטי לממשלה אין בסיס סטטוטורי מפורש — היא נבנתה מהכרזתו-העצמית של היועמ”ש שמגר (1968) שתפקידו “מעין-שיפוטי”, התגבשה בפסיקת השופט ברק בפרשת דרעי–פנחסי (1993) שלפיה חוות-דעת היועמ”ש “משקפת את הדין המצוי”, והופעלה דרך החלטות ממשלה. לכך נוסף מונופול הייצוג: היועמ”ש קובע אילו טיעונים תציג מדינת ישראל בבית המשפט. אם הוא אומר שעמדת הממשלה “אינה ראויה להגנה”, לממשלה שתי אפשרויות בלבד — לנטוש את עמדתה, או להופיע בשמה-שלה עם עורך-דין פרטי, ללא מערך משפטי, מול היועמ”ש שלה. בנוסף, יועצים משפטיים במשרדי הממשלה — הכפופים מקצועית ליועמ”ש ולא לשר — יכולים לסרב לנסח חוות-דעת התומכת בהחלטת שר שהם רואים כבלתי-חוקית, והשר אינו יכול לפטרם. המבנה כולו — המעניק לגורם ממונה כוח וטו על אסטרטגיית הממשלה — נשען על פרשנות שיפוטית והחלטות ממשלה, לא על חוק שהכנסת חוקקה.

4. הרכב המותב — מי קובע אילו שופטים ידונו בתיק

מה כבר קרה. בג”ץ 5658/23, שבו בוטל תיקון לחוק-יסוד, נדון בהרכב מורחב של 15 שופטים — והוכרע ברוב של 8 מול 7. גודל ההרכב הוא שקבע את התוצאה: בהרכב קטן יותר היה הרוב עשוי להתהפך.

המנגנון. ברירת המחדל היא שיבוץ ממוחשב, אך לנשיא/ה בית המשפט נתון שיקול-דעת בלעדי בקביעת גודל ההרכב — והרחבתו “לכל מספר אי-זוגי” בעניינים בעלי חשיבות מיוחדת. כפי שמציינת ביקורת אקדמית (Federalist Society Review), בחירה בהרכב של שבעה במקום חמישה יכולה להכריע תוצאה בתיק חוקתי טעון. מחקר אמפירי (Givati & Rosenberg, Journal of Empirical Legal Studies, 2020) מצא כי שופטים שהנשיא/ה המכהן/ת בחר/ה בעבר יושבים עמו/ה בהרכבים בתדירות גבוהה יותר — ממצא העולה בקנה אחד עם הרכבה אסטרטגית. כלומר, שיקול-דעת בעל השפעה על התוצאה בתיקים הרגישים ביותר מרוכז בידי גורם אחד. (אין מדובר בטענה שהנשיא בורר כל מותב; ברירת המחדל אקראית, וההשפעה היא דרך גודל ההרכב והחריג.)

5. היעדר זכות עמידה — כל אחד, על כמעט הכל

מה כבר קרה. ב-2006 פסל בג”ץ את “חוק טל” (דחיית שירות לתלמידי ישיבות) בעתירת התנועה למען איכות השלטון — ארגון חברה אזרחית שאין לחבריו כל פגיעה אישית מהֶסדר הגיוס. חקיקה שנתמכה בקואליציה שלטת בוטלה בעתירת גוף ללא שום נגיעה אישית לעניין. אותו הסדר נפסל שוב ושוב לאחר מכן (2012, 2017, 2024), בכל פעם בעתירות של ארגונים ללא פגיעה אישית. (מקור: בג”ץ 6427/02; VERSA, Cardozo.)

המנגנון. בבג”ץ 910/86 רסלר נ’ שר הביטחון (1988) צמצם השופט ברק את דרישת זכות-העמידה כמעט עד לאיונה, וברא מסלול מקביל: גם ללא פגיעה אישית, מוקנית עמידה כאשר העניין הוא בעל “חשיבות ציבורית מיוחדת” ונוגע לשלטון החוק. כפי שמציין פרויקט VERSA, בית המשפט “סילק למעשה את [זכות העמידה] כסף כניסה”. התוצאה מבנית: ארגונים מאורגנים וממומנים יכולים להשתמש בבג”ץ כמנגנון ביקורת-מדיניות מתמשך נגד כל ממשלה — והרוב הפרלמנטרי אינו מגן, שכן בית המשפט עצמו הוא שמכריע אם מתקיימת עמידה.

6. בחירה-עצמית של השופטים — והמקרה ששמו על הפרויקט

מה כבר קרה. שר המשפטים יריב לוין ביקש למנות לבית המשפט העליון את אביעד בקשי ואת רפאל ביטון — שניים מן הדמויות שזוהו עם רפורמת 2023. בהיעדר הרוב המיוחס הנדרש (7 מתוך 9) למינוי המבוקש, נוצר קיפאון: השר נמנע מלכנס את הוועדה כדי למַנות שופטים אחרים, ושלוש משרות בעליון נותרו ריקות לאורך זמן. התוצאה כללה מחסור חמור בשופטים בכל מערכת בתי המשפט. (הקיפאון היה דו-צדדי: גם סירוב השר לכנס את הוועדה וגם הצבעת חברי הוועדה נגד המועמדים תרמו לו. מקורות: דיווחי Times of Israel; IDI.) שמו של פרויקט זה — בקשי וביטון — לקוח ממקרה זה.

המנגנון. הוועדה לבחירת שופטים מונה תשעה חברים, ובהם שלושה שופטי עליון. מאז 2008 נדרש רוב מיוחס של 7 מתוך 9 למינוי שופטי עליון. האסימטריה מבנית: כדי לחסום מועמד די בחמישה קולות; כדי למַנות דרושים שבעה. לאורך זמן, מבנה זה מושך את הרכב בית המשפט אל מועמדים המקובלים על השופטים היושבים, ולא בהכרח על נבחרי הציבור של אותה עת. כך משכפלת השכבה את עצמה ומקטינה את משקלם של נבחרי הציבור בהרכבתה. (ב-2025 בוטל מבנה הרוב המיוחס בחקיקה השנויה במחלוקת, המעוכבת ועומדת לביקורת חוקתית בפני בית המשפט עצמו.)

7. בית המשפט קובע את גבולות סמכותו-שלו — “הכל שפיט”

מה כבר קרה. ב-1 בינואר 2024 פסל בית המשפט, בהרכב מלא של 15 שופטים, את תיקון מס’ 3 לחוק-יסוד: השפיטה — התיקון שביקש לבטל את עילת הסבירות לגבי החלטות הדרג הנבחר. ברוב 12–3 קבע בית המשפט עקרונית את סמכותו לבחון תיקונים לחוקי-יסוד; וברוב 8–7 פסל את התיקון הספציפי. כלומר: ניסיון הכנסת לצמצם את סמכות הביקורת של בית המשפט בוטל בידי בית המשפט עצמו, מכוח תאוריית-סמכות שבית המשפט קבע לעצמו, וללא נתיב ערעור או התגברות. (הפסיקה במאגר זה: בג”ץ 5658/23.)

המנגנון. דוקטרינת “הכל שפיט” של השופט ברק, יחד עם היפוך דוקטרינת “השאלה הפוליטית” בפרשת רסלר (1988), הרחיבה את תחום הביקורת כך שכמעט כל עניין — לרבות החלטות מדיניות, ביטחון ומינויים שנחשבו בעבר “פוליטיים” — ניתן לבירור שיפוטי. המאפיין המבני: אין גוף חיצוני שיכול לקבוע לבית המשפט שאין לו סמכות. בית המשפט מכריע מה שפיט, והכרעותיו בעניין השפיטוּת אינן ניתנות לביקורת. זהו מנגנון חד-כיווני: בית המשפט הרחיב את היקף השפיטוּת, וכאשר הכנסת ניסתה לצמצמו בחקיקה — ביטל את הצמצום מכוח סמכותו להעביר תחת שבט ביקורתו אף חוקי-יסוד.

הערה מתודולוגית

תיאורי המנגנונים לעיל הם עובדות פרוצדורליות הניתנות לאימות מן הבסיס המשפטי, מן הפסיקה ומאופן פעולת המערכת; כל תוצאה מובאת עם מקורהּ. הקביעה שמדובר בריכוז כוח ובהיחלשות שלטון נבחרי הציבור היא הערכה של הפרויקט — דעה המעוגנת בעובדות הגלויות שלצידה, ולא טענת עובדה על מצב נפשי או מניע של אדם מסוים. במכוון נמנענו מהכללת-יתר השוואתית (“אין לכך אח ורע בעולם”): העובדה המבנית עצמה נושאת את הטיעון. הדמויות הנזכרות נזכרות אך ורק בהקשר של מעשיהן הפומביים והמתועדים בתפקידן הציבורי.
structure.body_html_en:
This chapter is not about a single ruling but about a structure: a set of procedural mechanisms through which decision-making power moved from elected officials to an unelected layer of judges and legal advisors. Each mechanism is presented in two parts — what already happened (the documented outcome it produced), then the mechanism (how it actually works, and its legal basis). The procedural facts are verifiable from the law and from how the system operates; the judgment that this amounts to a concentration of power is the project’s assessment, anchored in the disclosed facts beside it.

1. The apex court as the court of first instance — and last

What already happened. On 18 January 2023 the High Court of Justice voided, 10–1, the appointment of Aryeh Deri as Minister of the Interior and of Health, holding it “unreasonable in the extreme.” Prime Minister Netanyahu said he was “compelled, with a heavy heart” to dismiss him, and did so by cabinet vote days later. The government had no avenue of appeal: the HCJ is both the first and the last judicial word. (The ruling, in this database: HCJ 8948/22 Deri.)

The mechanism. Section 15 of Basic Law: The Judiciary provides that the Supreme Court also sits as the High Court of Justice, hearing petitions against state authorities directly. The same body is thus both the state’s final court of appeal and the first-instance forum where a citizen challenges a minister’s or government’s decision — with no appeal from its decision. In every other democracy with judicial review of executive action, the first-instance ruling can be appealed to a higher court that corrects error and moderates a single bench’s preferences. In Israel the HCJ is the Supreme Court: the government faces a single adjudication, before the most powerful bench in the country, with no further legal recourse. The Court did the same on 1 January 2024, when a full bench of 15 justices reviewed and struck down an amendment to a Basic Law (see §7) — also at first and last instance.

2. Direct access to the apex — bypassing the lower courts

What already happened. In August 2019, after the justice minister fired the ministry’s director-general and moved to install a political associate, a petition was filed directly with the HCJ, and within days the appointment was frozen by interim order. No administrative tribunal or district-court step preceded it, and no appeal was possible — the HCJ was the only forum from the first moment. (Source: Jerusalem Post and Haaretz reporting, August 2019.)

The mechanism. Petitions on the most significant national matters — government decisions, regulations, appointments, legislation — go straight to the Supreme Court. The 2000 Administrative Courts Law moved enumerated categories to district courts, but everything not expressly listed stays with the HCJ via a catch-all clause. In most legal systems a challenger must exhaust remedies, pass a specialist tribunal, a general court, and an appellate tier — and the government governs throughout. In Israel, for the retained categories, there is no exhaustion requirement and no lower-court filter: an appointment announced Monday can be frozen by the HCJ by Friday. The government cannot use procedural time as a political resource — which also amplifies the Attorney General’s power (§3), since the threat of rapid intervention makes the AG’s opinion costly to ignore.

3. The chain of legal advisors — binding opinions, not advice

What already happened. In 2025 the Attorney General announced she would not represent the government in petitions concerning the Prime Minister’s refusal to establish a state commission of inquiry into the events of 7 October, and that the government would have to seek its own private counsel. The government’s own legal arm opposed its position, and the Prime Minister had to find private lawyers to appear in the country’s highest court — against his own Attorney General. (Source: Times of Israel and Ynet reporting, 2025.)

The mechanism. The Attorney General’s binding authority has no explicit statutory basis — it was built from AG Shamgar’s 1968 self-declaration that the role is “of a judicial nature,” crystallized in Justice Barak’s 1993 Deri-Pinhasi ruling that the AG’s opinion “reflects the positive law,” and operationalized through government resolutions. To this is added a monopoly on representation: the AG decides what arguments the State of Israel presents in court. If the AG deems the government’s position “indefensible,” the government’s only options are to abandon it, or to appear in its own name with private counsel — with no legal apparatus, against its own AG. Ministry legal advisors — professionally subordinate to the AG, not to the minister — can refuse to draft an opinion supporting a decision they deem unlawful, and the minister cannot dismiss them. The entire structure — granting an appointed official a veto over the government’s legal strategy — rests on judicial interpretation and government resolutions, not on a statute the Knesset enacted.

4. Panel composition — who decides which justices hear the case

What already happened. HCJ 5658/23, in which an amendment to a Basic Law was struck down, was heard by an expanded panel of 15 justices — and decided 8 to 7. The size of the panel determined the outcome: on a smaller panel the majority might have flipped.

The mechanism. Assignment is computerized by default, but the Court President holds sole discretion over panel size — expanding it “to any odd number” in matters of particular importance. As academic critique notes (Federalist Society Review), choosing seven justices instead of five can decide a charged constitutional case. An empirical study (Givati & Rosenberg, Journal of Empirical Legal Studies, 2020) found that justices a sitting President had earlier selected sit on her panels more often — consistent with strategic composition. Outcome-affecting discretion in the most sensitive cases is thus concentrated in one office. (This is not a claim that the President hand-picks every panel; the default is random, and the effect runs through panel size and the exception.)

5. No standing requirement — anyone, on almost anything

What already happened. In 2006 the HCJ struck down the “Tal Law” (military-service deferment for yeshiva students) on a petition by the Movement for Quality Government — a civic NGO whose members suffer no personal injury from the deferment arrangement. Legislation backed by a governing coalition was voided on the petition of a body with no personal stake. The same arrangement was struck down again and again afterward (2012, 2017, 2024), each time on petitions by organizations with no personal injury. (Source: HCJ 6427/02; VERSA, Cardozo.)

The mechanism. In HCJ 910/86 Ressler v. Minister of Defence (1988), Justice Barak reduced the standing requirement almost to nothing and created a parallel track: even without personal injury, standing is granted where the matter is of “particular public importance” and touches the rule of law. As VERSA notes, the Court “effectively removed [standing] as a threshold.” The result is structural: organized, well-funded organizations can use the HCJ as a continuous policy-review mechanism against any government — and the parliamentary majority provides no shield, because the Court itself decides whether standing is met.

6. Judicial self-selection — and the case the project is named after

What already happened. Justice Minister Yariv Levin sought to appoint to the Supreme Court Aviad Bakshi and Raphael Bitton — two figures identified with the 2023 reform. Lacking the required supermajority (7 of 9) for those appointments, a deadlock set in: the minister declined to convene the committee to appoint other justices, and three Supreme Court seats stood vacant for an extended period, contributing to a severe judge shortage across the court system. (The deadlock was two-sided: both the minister’s refusal to convene and the committee members’ votes against the candidates produced it. Sources: Times of Israel; IDI.) This project’s name — Bakshi & Bitton — is taken from this episode.

The mechanism. The Judicial Selection Committee has nine members, three of them sitting Supreme Court justices. Since 2008 a supermajority of 7 of 9 is required to appoint Supreme Court justices. The asymmetry is structural: blocking a candidate takes only five votes; appointing one takes seven. Over time this draws the Court’s composition toward candidates acceptable to the sitting justices, not necessarily to the elected government of the day — the layer reproducing itself and diminishing elected officials’ weight in its composition. (In 2025 the supermajority structure was changed by contested legislation, now deferred and under constitutional review by the Court itself.)

7. The Court defines the limits of its own jurisdiction — “everything is justiciable”

What already happened. On 1 January 2024 the Court, sitting as a full bench of 15, struck down Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary — the amendment that sought to remove reasonableness review of elected-branch decisions. By 12–3 it held, in principle, that it has authority to review amendments to Basic Laws; by 8–7 it voided this specific amendment. The Knesset’s attempt to narrow the Court’s review power was thus annulled by the Court itself, under a theory of jurisdiction the Court devised for itself, with no avenue of appeal or override. (The ruling, in this database: HCJ 5658/23.)

The mechanism. Justice Barak’s “everything is justiciable” doctrine, together with the reversal of the “political question” doctrine in Ressler (1988), expanded review so that almost any matter — including policy, security, and appointment decisions once treated as “political” — is open to judicial review. The structural feature: there is no external body that can tell the Court it lacks jurisdiction. The Court decides what is justiciable, and its justiciability rulings are not themselves reviewable. It is a one-way ratchet: the Court expanded justiciability, and when the Knesset tried to narrow it by statute, the Court voided the narrowing under its self-declared power to review even Basic Laws.

A note on method

The descriptions of the mechanisms above are procedural facts, verifiable from the legal basis, the case law, and how the system operates; each outcome is given with its source. The judgment that this amounts to a concentration of power and a weakening of elected government is the project’s assessment — an opinion anchored in the disclosed facts beside it, not a factual claim about any individual’s state of mind or motive. We have deliberately avoided the sweeping comparative claim (“nothing like it anywhere”): the structural fact itself carries the argument. The figures named here are named solely in connection with their public, documented acts in their official roles.
